Hi Shaman,

Thanks again for helping root out possible bugs in dRep, and I'm happy to hear that your lab is finding it useful.

That error is the result of it not finding any Mash runs for whatever reason. A couple of things:

1) It looks like your command might not be in the correct format; a work directory (where all of the output is stored) is supposed to be immediately after the call to `derepliate_wf`. For example:

```
$ dRep dereplicate_wf /scratch/users/snarayanasamy/LAO_TS_IMP-v1.3/TemporalBinning/RepresentativeBins -p 4 --completeness 0.6 --strain_htr 101 --P_ani 0.6 --S_ani 0.965 --overwrite --run_tax -g /scratch/users/snarayanasamy/LAO_TS_IMP-v1.3/TemporalBinning/AllBins/*.fa
```

The output folder would then be:

```
/scratch/users/snarayanasamy/LAO_TS_IMP-v1.3/TemporalBinning/RepresentativeBins
```

2) The program is able to test itself to see if the environment is causing issues. If you download the "tests" folder, and run the following command from within it:

```
$ ./test_suite.py
```

it should automatically run dRep on the test genomes in a couple of different ways, and automatically compare the results to some pre-computed output.

3) To troubleshoot the issue you're reporting above (assuming that the order of the arguments in the call is not what's causing the problem), there are a couple of things that would be helpful to know:

- could you upload your log? (log/logger.log)
- are there any files in your Mash data folder? (data/MASH_files/sketches/)
- could you upload Bdb? (data_tables/Bdb.csv). That data-table lists all the genomes the program is using as input.

Best,
-Matt

Dunno how serious we are about taking Curio to other programming communities, but if we are here is an example of a project that did that: [docopt](https://github.com/docopt)

Docopt started off as an idea in Python about how to design a command-line argument parser. People liked the idea so much that they took it to other languages. So in addition to the original [Python docopt](https://github.com/docopt/docopt), there is [docopt.rs](https://github.com/docopt/docopt.rs), [docopt.cpp](https://github.com/docopt/docopt.cpp), [docopt.net](https://github.com/docopt/docopt.net), etc. Each is a separate repo under a [single docopt organization](https://github.com/docopt).

Note that `clock` and `gettimeofday` system calls actually are virtual system calls, and are pretty fast (even with CPython wrappers).

```
In [4]: def f(): pass

In [5]: %timeit f()
The slowest run took 34.07 times longer than the fastest. This could mean that an intermediate result is being cached.
10000000 loops, best of 3: 90.8 ns per loop

In [6]: %timeit time.monotonic()
The slowest run took 25.96 times longer than the fastest. This could mean that an intermediate result is being cached.
10000000 loops, best of 3: 115 ns per loop

In [7]: t = time.monotonic

In [8]: %timeit t()
The slowest run took 31.04 times longer than the fastest. This could mean that an intermediate result is being cached.
10000000 loops, best of 3: 81.3 ns per loop

In [9]: i = 0

In [10]: %timeit i + 1
The slowest run took 63.23 times longer than the fastest. This could mean that an intermediate result is being cached.
10000000 loops, best of 3: 43 ns per loop
```
Calling `time.monotonic` is cheaper than calling an empty python function!

I have implemented a major reworking on cancellation control and related features.  Rather than describe it here, please read this:

http://curio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/devel.html#cancellation-control

This change will break all existing use of {defer|allow}_cancellation (sorry). There are comparable features with {disable|enable}_cancellation() functions though.  It should not break other Curio code so far as I know. 

Basically gist is this:  cancellation can still be controlled, but there are some refined rules about when things happen, handling of exceptions, and nesting.

Not every combination of digits that makes up zipcode results in a valid zipcode. Zipcodes are pretty much arbitrary numbers associate with geographical boundaries (or, in some cases, companies). 

The only way to just have valid zipcodes would be to choose at random from a list of known valid zipcodes, instead of generating them from an algorithm.

I'm afraid that including such a list of valid zipcodes for each provider may result in a maintenance burden. Zipcodes are constantly added and removed in various country, and we can't possibly keep up with that.

That's one of the reason why my latest efforts are focused on #90 and #178 . With those in place, you could use your own custom provider tailored to your specific needs.

@mohitmessi007 Assuming Linux or MacOS, this `bash` script might help. Runs your plugin using all cores available.
```bash
PLUGIN_NAME=<your_plugin_name>
NUM_PROCS=0
UNAME="$(uname -s)"
case "${UNAME}" in
    Linux*)     NUM_PROCS=$(grep -c ^processor /proc/cpuinfo);;
    Darwin*)    NUM_PROCS=$(sysctl -n hw.ncpu);;
esac
printf "Using ${NUM_PROCS} processes.\n"
find . -name "*.yang" | xargs -P $NUM_PROCS -n 1 -I {} bash -c 'cd $(dirname {}) && pyang -f $PLUGIN_NAME $(basename {}) > $(basename {}).$PLUGIN_NAME'
```

`setup.py` can also be run from `sdist` in that case it will be in the root of a unpacked zip archive that pip unzips in some temporary location. So it cannot e.g. copy from `../../dlib/*` to `src`. The solution would be somehow distinguish this from when `setup.py` is running from a git repo, at the same time it should always try to keep the `src` and actual repository in sync. Another solution is to detect if it is inside a git repo (has `.git`) pull the repository to `src`. In any case, it can be done but would be fragile because it falls outside of the usual usage.

Yes Python is slow, that is why I almost never run any tight loops in Python. For the most parts I use `numpy`/`scipy` or a good library like `dlib` or `scikit` to do the heavy lifting and glue them in Python. Most algorithms get enough advantage from this vectorization that they are good enough. When absolutely required I do it in Cython which is basically running C/C++ from Python.

The advantage of Python is that I can quickly get something, and save on development time, if the solution is working but slow will make it faster by doing it in C.

Ok, starting to get to the bottom of this.

The whole thing boils down to this: Depending on which `InteroperabilityIndex` is present (frequently that is `R98`), additional fields exist in the IOP IFD, as specified here:

http://www.exif.org/dcf.PDF

this includes the field `InteroperabilityVersion`, which defines the version of the R98 standard (currently 1.0), among other fields.

To avoid having to implement all possible additional fields that may be present given different interoperability standards, piexif should retain unknown fields using bare data and not discard them.

The reason keyring has so many releases is fourfold:

1. The release process has been simplified to a "tag & push" process, so it's so lightweight there's no intrinsic impediment to making releases.
2. As it's impossible to anticipate when the _next_ release is going to occur, it's difficult to withhold releases artificially. That is, a small change might come and seem unworthy of a release, but then might sit for several months awaiting another indication for a push. To avoid that, I prefer to publish releases as soon as they're ready.
3. Releasing early and often means that any suitably stable change can be released quickly and not be bundled with other changes, allowing for regressions to be detected and corrected more quickly, and often without intervening changes.
4. By releasing every version of the software, it's easy for anyone to bisect changes and adopt features without needing to check out the source code (simply using `pip install` for example).

It's very rare that I'll make a release knowing that another release is imminent. I generally only do that if I want to be sure to create a gap between deprecation of a feature and removal of the interface.

@prometheanfire I certainly don't want to create a hassle for you. If there's something that keyring can do to help automate the downstream release process and make it less of a chore, I'm happy to help support that. At the very least, I don't expect that downstream packages will necessarily adopt each release, but should follow whatever cadence works best for their environment and use case. Because Gentoo has access to every published release, it also has the control to select which releases to incorporate or not, but of course has the same problem I do -- not knowing whether the release yesterday will be followed by another contrib and release tomorrow.

I like @mitya57's guidance - incorporate relevant releases as resources permit and disregard the irrelevant ones. Consider delaying releases by a few weeks or making timed releases to limit the frequency of them. You have that control.

I hope that helps and gives some context. I hope that answers your questions, so I'll close this ticket, but don't hesitate to make suggestions or follow up with questions if there's any open concerns.

Coincidentally I was looking into this type of thing the other day. As you've mentioned, and as far as I understand, tcl does not support UTF-16, but you can still use UTF-8, so you'd have to convert your characters to that format:

```python
from tkinter import Tk, messagebox
root = Tk()
root.tk.call('encoding', 'system', 'utf-8')
messagebox.showinfo("UTF-8 example", u'\ud83d\udd08')
```

The documentation left a lot to be desired in this area. On first impressions it sounded like it should be able to support 16-bit Unicode by default: https://www.tcl.tk/man/tcl/TclCmd/encoding.htm#M10

However it seems like their 16 bit implementation is UCS-16: https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Tcl_Programming/Internationalization#Unicode_implementations:_UTF-8.2C_UCS-16
Which I personally don't know much about. Technically speaking UTF-16 derived from UCS-2, but I honestly don't know where this "UCS-16" comes into play.
Funnily enough internally it is declared as "unicode" so trying `root.tk.call('encoding', 'system', 'ucs-16')` won't work and you'll have to use `root.tk.call('encoding', 'system', 'unicode')` instead.

I can't find any documentation that gives a clear answer.  My best guess is that the ```write()``` method is intended to deliberately hide the underlying protocol.

By escaping the IAC characters, they prevent users from breaking/interfering with the connection by unintentionally sending protocol control commands.  None of the other methods provide a mechanism for a user to send commands of their own, leaving all control responsibility to the class.

I think we should be ok if we circumvent this only in the case that we want to send a NOP as the NOP is benign, and any unexpected control response from the host would still be properly processed by the class read methods.

I didn't check the actual implementation of chanselect and its thread-related semantics, but I like the general approach, when you pass getters and putters, and get channel that's ready.

The use of context here looks promising. Though the arguments of select must be operations (get/put) on channels, not just channels.

There's also [another attempt](https://github.com/rgalanakis/goless) at bringing Go-like functionality to Python. They took [`reflect.Select`](https://golang.org/pkg/reflect/#Select) for reference and implemented it in Python.

I tried to convert an [example](https://golang.org/ref/spec#Select_statements) from golang specification:

```python
c = Channel()
c1 = Channel()
c2 = Channel()
c3 = Channel()
i1, i2 = 0, 0

ch, value, ok = await select(c1.get(), c2.put(i2), c3.get())
if ch is c1:
    i1 = value
    print("received", i1, "from c1")
elif ch is c2:
    print("sent", i2, "to c2")
elif ch is c3:
    i3 = value
    if ok:
        print("received", i3, "from c3")
    else:
        print("c3 is closed")
else:
    print("no communication")
```

Methods `get()` and `put(v)` return Future subclasses that hold references to corresponding channels. Select must raise an error if *c2* has been closed prior, Go program panics in this case. *ch* is None when no channel are ready, i.e. default case, *value* is result of `get()`, *ok* is True when an actual value was received from channel, *ok* is False when channel was closed.

Docs of `reflect.Select` state that *ok* is "a boolean indicating whether the value corresponds to a send on the channel (as opposed to a zero value received because the channel is closed)." I'm very ashamed, but I don't quite get this passage :smile:

Back to the state of bss_eval, I want trigger some discussion about what we could contribute to mir_eval and what would be the possible use cases, which is mainly the [SISEC Evaluation](https://sisec.inria.fr/home/2016-professionally-produced-music-recordings/) organised by @aliutkus this year.

#### Current implementation

* is a direct implementation of [bss_eval_sources v 3.0](http://bass-db.gforge.inria.fr/bss_eval/bss_eval_sources.m)

#### Issues/Missing features

##### Multichannel support 

Since many separation methods are now evaluating at least stereo mixes, supporting the `Image to Spatial distortion Ratio` would increase the usability of mir_eval a lot, since the current SISEC dataset is stereo and in evaluated using bss_eval_images

My student @ErikJohnsonCU and I have a working bss_eval_images pure python implementation which we could make ready for a PR into mir_eval. However, one of the issues that would need to be solved first, would be:

* how to do the unit tests regarding the test vectors which are mono. I would propose not add more test vectors to the repository, but instead generate fake stereo files from the existing mono files. 

* We can also add new json files for matlab compatibility comparisons

* @craffel how would you add the bss_eval_images? as a separate function or make a wrapper so that users can decide if they want the evaluate the separation on `sources` or `images`?

##### Frame wise evaluation

The [SISEC matlab based evaluation](https://github.com/faroit/dsd100mat) evaluates the audio signal frame wise instead of globally for the whole signal. We could add this to both the existing `sources` as well as to the new `images`. This would solve #192.

##### Speed

`bss_eval_sources.m` is slow. The mir_eval version is even slower. Would you be interested in a cython, numba... optimised version or would you prefer to have as few external dependencies as possible, hence simpler to install?

### DSD100

This year we will also have some [python tools for dsd100](https://github.com/faroit/dsd100py) which parses the dataset and makes it easy for python users to participate in SISEC. However we cannot use mir_eval for the official evaluation since multichannel support is missing. Therefore it would be really cool if we can get the `images` support into within the next few weeks ;-)

The `pair_to_pair` one is failing because `BedTools` reports two consecutive tabs near the end of each line in the output (shown here in the the "expected" version):
```
Got:
chr1\t100\t200\tchr5\t5000\t5100\tbedpe_example1\t30\t+\t-\tchr5\t5000\t5100\tchr1\t1000\t2000\tbedpe_example1\t-\t+\n

When we read the file contents (as was happening prior to this PR), the double tabs are retained. But when the fields are split in `cbedtools.py` during parsine, only one tab is retained. I believe this is on the Cython side. It remains to be decided whether this is a bug or not.

I am working on this, please refer to [my fork](https://github.com/burnpanck/pycparser/tree/feature-extract-comments). In fact, I do not intend to handle comments in the parser itself, but rather try to correlate comments stripped out by the preprocessor with the `coords` of the generated AST. The reason is, it is not at all clear to which node a particular comment should be attached, as there is no standard specifying anything about the comments. Rather, the consumer of the correlated AST and comments will have to decide how to use these comments: Typically, a comment applies to *the next thing* afterwards unless it is on the same line, but that might not be true in all cases.

In my fork, I try to provide character-exact coordinate ranges to each AST node. What I'm currently struggling with, is that for some AST nodes, it is not at all clear to me, what would be the proper range. Generally, I'd like to supply the range that encloses all tokens that are described by the AST node, such that the range for the variable declaration `int a;` includes both the type `int` and the identifier `a` (but probably not the semicolon). However, in a declaration like `int a /* this is variable a*/,b;`, two `Decl` nodes are produced, one for each of the two variables. In order to be able to associate the comment with the variable `a`, the `Decl` node for `b` should be limited to only the identifier. Unfortunately, the `name` attribute of `Decl` is not an AST node itself, so there is no space to store the range for just the name to. What are your thoughts on this?

The main problem I have with cgroups is that I don't use/know them and it looks like a pretty extended subject. Before a PR I would like to see a discussion about what capabilities should be implemented and what the API should look like.

The main question is: should psutil be a fully featured cgroups library? My hunch is "no", because that would be too much and outside of the scope of psutil as a project. 

For instance, the API of libs like this one: 
https://github.com/cloudsigma/cgroupspy
...gives me the sensation that this cgroup thing is something big which deserves a separate, dedicated project. I don't want something like that in psutil.

On the other hand, something like this:
https://github.com/francisbouvier/cgroups
...intrigues me because it seems it could be relatively easy to associate a cgroup to a PID, and that looks like something which could be integrated in `psutil.Process` class somehow.

As to how to do that in terms of API, I'm not sure, and that's because I'm ignorant about how cgroups work exactly. Judging from https://github.com/francisbouvier/cgroups it seems they are bound to a name: you first create a cgroup, then you associate a PID to it. That's kind of a bummer because it requires a separate `Cgroup(name)` class of some sort.
That's why I'm calling for others who are more familiar than me with cgroups to think about a simple API and start a discussion.

> Right, so my original proposal was to rename it to something like `__PrimePoint`, which should name mangle it and make it so it isn't accessible, right?

It would still be accessible, but not easily. I'm not sure about @tiran's intentions. Remove the public interface or remove the code?

> I don't understand what asn1crypto is doing in this scenario that is leaking the private key.

It uses `public_key` to calculate the public key from the private key. If the implementation can be attacked through side channel attacks (and I believe it can), the private key can be reconstructed.

> Executing the same pow() function repeatedly won't follow different branches, so no meaningful information is revealed.

Basically a simple square-and-multiply algorithm works like this:

```python3
def bits(n):
    while n:
        yield n % 2
        n //= 2

def powmod(a, b, m):
    """Calculate (a ** b) % m"""
    r = 1

    for n in bits(b):
        if n:
            r = r * a % m
        a = a * a % m

    return result
```

For each bit of `b`, the inner operation is either executed (1-bit) or not (0-bit). Those are different branches. Depending on the side channel available, it might be possible to see the difference between `a = a * a % m` and `r = r * a % m`. The total time of the whole computation won't help. But power consumption over time should do the trick. And there are cpu-cache attacks which I think of as magic. They should work too. A simple improvement on above implementation would be to add an `else:` clause doing an operation on a second variable.

The ECC case is worse. For every bit you do a "double" operation. And depending on the private bits you do "plus" operations or you don't. Those are two quite different operations and probably much easier to distinguish from each other.

I'm certainly no expert on side channel attacks. I know some theory about them, but I never tried to mount one. What I do know is that they are real and that implementing crypto math is usually a bad idea, at least if private keys are involved.

Would be great if an actual expert could confirm the above or correct me. I'm not in contact with any experts.

Of course. There are a semi-infinite number of ways of indexing data. ;)

More to the point, I understand that there are other ways to do it, but this is currently the way rom does it. Not to say it couldn't change in the future, but there is a lot of data currently being stored in this format that would need to be migrated. Migrations are (relatively) expensive with rom.

> Now you may contrast ...

Indeed, that is a different way of storing data. Mechanically, I can't really replace rom's existing data storage methods without forcing a lot of people to do substantial migrations. And thinking about what data is stored where in this format vs rom, I'm not sure if any of the data would end up unchanged, except for primary key column values, and maybe indexes (which would be renamed).

Also (and again), because rom index data can be completely manufactured, and is commonly not the same as the column data, we always need a reference to a list of index keys that a given "row" generates, so we know how to clean it up. As stated before, this could be placed with row data, but is currently placed in a large hash.

Hi Emir,
TBH, don't much have experience with XML dumps. I know that `pywikibot` supports downloading and reading them.
Specifically, there is [download_dump.py](https://phabricator.wikimedia.org/diffusion/PWBC/browse/master/scripts/maintenance/download_dump.py), and `xmlreader.py`.

The downloader is mostly a standalone script which should be easy to work with. As for the xml reader, working with it should be as easy as:

```python
import wikitextparser as wtp
from pywikibot import xmlreader
dump = xmlreader.XmlDump(r'path/to/xml/dump.xml')
dump_gen = dump.parse()
for entry in dump_gen:
    wikitext = entry.text
    parsed_wikitext = wtp.parse(wikitext)  # returns WikiText objects
    ...
```
Of-coarse pywikibot might be an overkill for your needs. Any XML reader like the one in Python's std library (`ElementTree`) or more advanced ones like `BeautifulSoup ` should do the job.

Replacing zero-length messages with newline characters is possible. We would need to create a local copy of such a LogRecord in order to preserve the original message intact and keep the output of other handlers correct. The same is technically true for sending a dict as a JSON dump: one might expect to see str(dict) or repr(dict) in the console, but would obtain JSON. I assume that it's not our intent to interfere with processing of zero-length messages or dicts by other handlers.

    def test_varchar_converter(self):
        """
        Ensure a conversion function can be used to force CHAR and VARCHAR columns
        to be returned as bytes instead of Unicode strings.
        """
        def tobyte(val):
            if val is None:
                return None
            return val.encode('ascii')

        self.cnxn.add_output_converter(pyodbc.SQL_CHAR, tobyte)
        self.cnxn.add_output_converter(pyodbc.SQL_VARCHAR, tobyte)
        self.cnxn.add_output_converter(pyodbc.SQL_LONGVARCHAR, tobyte)

        value = b'testing'
        self.cursor.execute("create table t1(s varchar(20))")
        self.cursor.execute("insert into t1 values (?)", value)

```

> I wonder if this is related to #213.

I don't believe so. In #213 the communication between the client and the server is correct; it's simply a matter of dealing with multiple calls involving parameters of varying lengths. This issue is about an apparent miscommunication between the client and the server, clearly related to the fact that some UTF-16LE encoded characters are represented by more than two (2) bytes.

As mentioned earlier, this issue does not occur on Windows clients, leading me to suspect that it is related to unixODBC. The issue was first reported as occurring with unixODBC 2.3.4, which is current, so it's not just a case of a Linux repository installing an old version (e.g., the Ubuntu repositories still seem to be stuck on 2.3.1). A follow-up post showed the ODBC trace from a Centos 7 box which included

```
[ODBC][2144][1490196227.260898][SQLBindParameter.c][217]
                Entry:
                        Statement = 0x2e64a00
                        Param Number = 1
                        Param Type = 1
                        C Type = -8 SQL_C_WCHAR
                        SQL Type = -9 SQL_WVARCHAR
                        Col Def = 20
                        Scale = 0
                        Rgb Value = 0x7f37219600a0
                        Value Max = 42
                        StrLen Or Ind = 0x2e38718
```

Comparing that to an ODBC trace under Windows ...

```
main            14e0-854    EXIT  SQLBindParameter  with return code 0 (SQL_SUCCESS)
        HSTMT               0x022C1A60
        UWORD                        1 
        SWORD                        1 <SQL_PARAM_INPUT>
        SWORD                       -8 <SQL_C_WCHAR>
        SWORD                       -9 <SQL_WVARCHAR>
        SQLULEN                   21
        SWORD                        0 
        PTR                0x01D64530
        SQLLEN                    42
        SQLLEN *            0x005C9454 (42)
```

... it looks like the Windows call is making allowances for the extra 2 bytes by declaring the parameter length as 21 "effective two-byte characters" (`SQLULEN 21`) while the Linux call appears to be specifying the actual number of Unicode characters (`Col Def = 20`). Perhaps related is the "magic number" 42 that appears in both cases (`Value Max = 42` under Linux, `SQLLEN 42` under Windows).

I guess the question is: Who is responsible for taking care of that bit of accounting? Does the ODBC spec say anything specific about Unicode encoding, especially with regard to UCS-2 (where every supported character is represented by exactly 2 bytes) versus UTF-16 (where some characters are represented by more than 2 bytes)?

It's used twice in `pretty_midi` as of right now; [once in `Instrument.get_piano_roll`](https://github.com/craffel/pretty-midi/blob/e3ab5f9f6b1cda4717fa6d24d3243460024bc949/pretty_midi/instrument.py#L105) and [once in PrettyMIDI.get_beats](https://github.com/craffel/pretty-midi/blob/bd97acd5d0373a8b120d0e0ae666145645171561/pretty_midi/pretty_midi.py#L550). The piano roll should be extended at least to the final sustain pedal, and get_beats should be extended at least to the last tempo change, so these both require more than the last end note.

> And in that case the method can give misleading values. Lyrics don't get synthesized and sustained notes will only last for so long, maybe 1 second. So lots of meta events happening 5, 10, or more seconds after the end of the last note would needlessly increase the size of the midi audio arrays.

If this is an issue then `get_end_time` shouldn't be used there; the one-liner should be used instead. `get_end_time` is defined unambiguously to be "the time of the last thing that happens in the MIDI object", and nothing else. If, for a specific use-case, it's more useful to know "the end time of the last note", or something similar, then a different function should be used instead. The goal of `pretty_midi` is to make such a function really easy to write, and I think the one-liner is evidence that this goal has been achieved here.

So, the Arlo API is built around a technology called EventStream. It was really designed for their web and mobile apps, and not for general scripting. As such, the EventStream is a stateful connection that acts similar to a websocket.

This library wraps the EventStream and provides a synchronous API, but under the hood it's subscribing to the EventStream in a thread when some methods are called. Some of the requests don't require the EventStream, and so they just make the HTTP request and return the response immediately. Others, make an HTTP request to the /notify endpoint, which triggers an asynchronous response via the EventStream. All of this is correlated using transaction ids.
The EventStream also requires a periodic keep-alive HTTP request to keep the EventStream open.

All that being said, I don't see how the library would be returning at line https://github.com/jeffreydwalter/arlo/blob/5956b1f3af30fdce7eea835aa3f9c6f250828aef/Arlo.py#L387 unless the timeout is occurring, which it really shouldn't in practice.

Thanks for tackling this! Two more things possibly worth considering:

1) Scaling with the square root of time is obviously only defined for log returns, whereas the calculation in place scales the ratio and hence the geometric returns used therein (agreed that for small returns the difference is only of academic interest. But it will become quite substantial for larger returns and larger time period transformations).

If you want to stick to geometric returns for the SR calculation, you could follow the procedure mentioned by benjaminmgross at [stackexchange](http://quant.stackexchange.com/questions/7290/computing-the-sharpe-ratio) (giving 5 precise steps in his comment below answer 1, Feb 16 '13 at 12:45). Also not computationally expensive.

2) make sure enough data points are used for meaningful std dev and hence a meaningful SR
 - use daily (ar at least monthly) returns instead of annual returns as default input ([Sharpe (1994)](http://web.stanford.edu/~wfsharpe/art/sr/SR.htm#Sharpe75) stated himself: "To maximize information content, it is usually desirable to measure risks and returns using fairly short (e.g. monthly) periods. For purposes of standardization it is then desirable to annualize the results.")
 - allow calculating the SR only if at least a certain number of data points is available (possibly 15)

Sorry it took me a bit to look at this.

The issue here seems to be based on the way that `XPathHelper` works. It assumes that valid entities in the tree register themselves such that we build an XML document that has the same structure as the YANG schema, but none of the values. Each element of this has an `obj_ptr` field that points to an entry in the `library` dict which is a keyed set of references to the actual pyangbind class.  There's a fairly complete explanation of this in [this document](http://pynms.io/pyangbind/xpath/).

When we don't initialise containers then this means that we end up with a structure that is not initialised in the tree - such that trying to retrieve a particular element that hasn't yet been initialised is causing a problem. For example, [this test](https://github.com/robshakir/pyangbind/blob/master/tests/xpath/01-list_leaflist/run.py#L195) will fail because in the tree at that point, there are actually no initialised items. That is, the tree looks like this:

```xml
<root obj_ptr="root"/>
```

Now this test is probably easier to fix, because we can just say -- if an element isn't initialised in the datatree then it shouldn't exist - i.e., the test can just initialise that object, which means that the tree changes how it looks and we get the expected test pass:

```python
  def test_get_list_retrieves_correct_attribute(self):
    self.instance.standalone.l.add(42)
    self.assertEqual(self.path_helper.get_list('/standalone/l')._yang_name, 'l')
``` 

And the tree that we see then:

```xml
<root obj_ptr="root">
  <standalone obj_ptr="2940b1fd-678f-11e8-b47f-9801a7ad8cb7">
    <l obj_ptr="2943c2d7-678f-11e8-a0fa-9801a7ad8cb7" x="42">
      <x obj_ptr="2943f287-678f-11e8-9066-9801a7ad8cb7"/>
    </l>
  </standalone>
</root>
```

Looking at it, this is basically what makes all of the tests fail - since the current expected behaviour is that we can find elements that don't exist in the tree.

The reason for this design was that if you build an API around the classes, then it's useful to be able to do (in pseudo-code):

```
obj = get object at /system/config
deserialise JSON from payload into obj
```

However, we could change the API we have to say:

```
obj = get-or-create object at /system/config
deserialise JSON from payload into obj
```

The new `get-or-create` would start at the root, and if it didn't find the entity there, it'd create it. This is relatively easy to add. All users would transition to this new `get-or-create` who needed this new behaviour. Alternatively, we can just make `get` implement `get-or-create`. I rather prefer the former (although it's backwards incompatible) because it maintains your memory savings.

The other test failure in the 01 XPATH tests looks to be a bug in the implementation, `init_code` ends up just trying to specify a referenced path. Let's discuss what you're trying to achieve there, since there are cases where the referenced path is inside a list. I think you can just remove that code, and things should work.

If we agree on this get-or-create type pattern, we can figure out how/where to implement this. Additionally, we can debug where the key references are going wrong. I imagine it's a very similar challenge (where the reference to `../config/keyname` is failing because we don't have the `config` container created to retrieve that leaf).

Sigh. You guys don't understand what 'pitch' is. It is not the same as width, nor number of pixels in gray. It is a memory offset. It is the same concept as what is called 'stride' in numpy lingo. (see https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.ctypes.html , or whatever else is available on numpy).

The idea is that computers are a lot more efficient when dealing with say, 4-bytes or 8-byte chunks. So when you want to faster-forward or backward in memory, you want to do so in such units, instead of bytes.
For bits, it is obvious that pitch is AT LEAST (the number of bits rounded up to multiple of 8)/8, since you can't fast-forward by half a byte. But for grays, you might have stride being width rounded up to multiple of 4, or 8, depending on whether you are on a 32-bit or a 64-bit platform.

Pitch is the distance between the two memory locations of the beginning of row1 and row2, etc. It is always larger than (bit-depth * pixel width) /8 , because memory locations like to be aligned to multiple of 4 or 8, depends on platform. i.e. if you have 17 pixels of gray per row, it is possible that stride can be 20 or 24.

Hi Sarah (@surdahl),

you've got two options in order to know the coverage time. The first is to use the SPICE utility program brief. You could download it from here:

https://naif.jpl.nasa.gov/naif/utilities.html

Pic your platform and click on it. That will lead you to a page where you can download **brief**

If you want to program it yourself, then:

1.  Use spiceypy.spkobj to get the IDs codes for all the ephemeris objects in the SPK file.
2. Use spiceypy.spkcov to get the coverage window for the ephemeris object of interest within the SPK file, e.g. spiceypy.spkcov( "de405.bsp", 499, cover ). **cover** contains now the ephemeris times you are looking for. Note that it is a SPICE window, i.e. it contains start and end times for each of the coverage intervals contained within the SPK file named "de405.bsp" for the object 499 (Mars)
3. Use spiceypy.timout to retrieve the string version for each of the time boundaries, e.g. timstrs = cspice_timout( cover, 'YYYY MON DD HR:MN:SC.### (TDB) ::TDB' )

If it helps, maybe you could check the Examples section of the **spkcov** API (it seems to be exactly what you are trying to do) from the Matlab toolkit:

https://naif.jpl.nasa.gov/pub/naif/toolkit_docs/MATLAB/mice/cspice_spkcov.html

Regards,
   Jorge

To access my profiles, I created my own detector factory:

    LOCAL_PROFILES_DIRECTORY = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), "profiles")

    _detector_factory = None


    def get_detector_factory():
        global _detector_factory
        if _detector_factory is None:
            _detector_factory = langdetect.DetectorFactory()
            _detector_factory.load_profile(LOCAL_PROFILES_DIRECTORY)
        return _detector_factory


    def detect_lang(text):
        u"""
        Try to detect the language of the text.

        >>> detect_lang(u"12 August 2016")
        u'et de en'

        >>> detect_lang(u"12. August 2016")
        u'et de en'

        >>> detect_lang(u"Questions écrites par les membres du Parlement européen")
        u'fr pt es en ro nl de it sv'

        >>> detect_lang(u"Αύξηση της ανεργίας στην Κύπρο")
        u'el'

        >>> detect_lang(u"Заседания от 18 до 21 май 2015 г.")
        u'bg'

        >>> detect_lang(u"2015. május 18–21-i ülések")
        u'hu es fr et cs sk sl'

        >>> detect_lang(u"2010. november 23.")  # 'hu' not detected
        u'da sl sv nl en'

        :type  text: str|unicode
        :param text: Text sample

        :rtype: str
        :return: whitespace-delimited Languages codes.
        """
        detector = get_detector_factory().create()

        detector.set_prior_map(MOST_SPOKEN_LANGUAGES)

        detector.PROB_THRESHOLD = .000001

        detector.seed = 0
        detector.append(text)
        try:
            languages = detector.get_probabilities()
        except langdetect.lang_detect_exception.LangDetectException:
            return ''
        else:
            return ' '.join(language.lang for language in languages)

**note:** I needed to get a list of languages, so my own function could return space-delimited list of languages codes.

In windows environments, I have run into similar problems and I only use the command line to call my python scripts.  Are you using `run` or `execfile()` command from the console to run your script?  Or are you copy and pasting individual modules into the console?    

I would reccomend using the format in the `test.py` script.  For example, if you only wanted to run the `.read` module:

````
import PyHum

def myfunction()
   # general settings   
   humfile = #your path 
   sonpath = #your path
   doplot = 1 #yes

   # reading specific settings
   cs2cs_args = "epsg:26949" #arizona central state plane
   bedpick = 1 # auto bed pick
   c = 1450 # speed of sound fresh water
   t = 0.108 # length of transducer
   draft = 0.3 # draft in metres
   flip_lr = 1 # flip port and starboard
   model = 998 # humminbird model
   cog = 1 # GPS course-over-ground used for heading
   calc_bearing = 0 #no
   filt_bearing = 0 #no
   chunk = 'd100' # distance, 100m
   ## read data in SON files into PyHum memory mapped format (.dat)
   PyHum.read(humfile, sonpath, cs2cs_args, c, draft, doplot, t, bedpick, flip_lr, model, calc_bearing, filt_bearing, cog, chunk)

if __name__ == '__main__':
  myfunction()
````

If you don't use the `if __name__ == '__main__':` wrapper, the script will recursively spawn subprocesses go into an infinite loop and use 100% of your computational resources.  This is windows problem and originates because it does not support forking of subprocesses.  If you have been using the wrapper, it could just be it has entered the parallel processing phase of the `.correct` module and it should finish eventually.

> I have an issue with sending an array with more than 255 characters length of total. Is there any other workaround other than specifying a range in excel?



Unfortunately not. This in an Excel limitation not an XlsxWriter limitation. From the [XlsxWriter docs](
https://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.io/working_with_data_validation.html?highlight=List):

> Note, when using the list validation with a list of strings, like in the last example above, Excel stores the strings internally as a Comma Separated Variable string. The total length for this string, including commas, cannot exceed the Excel limit of 255 characters. For longer sets of data you should use a range reference like the prior example above.

Thought I could get rid of an old compatibility hack for something that's been fixed in the C tools since 2015. Maybe I could add back a light version of it though...

Could you try this patch and see if it fixes the kernels you run? That syntax quirk actually affects more than just ``help`` tokens, but maybe it's enough.

```diff
diff --git a/kconfiglib.py b/kconfiglib.py
index e5db25c..5a5f28d 100644
--- a/kconfiglib.py
+++ b/kconfiglib.py
@@ -1646,8 +1646,17 @@ class Kconfig(object):
         # to the previous token. See _STRING_LEX for why this is needed.
         token = _get_keyword(match.group(1))
         if not token:
-            # If the first token is not a keyword, we have a preprocessor
-            # variable assignment (or a bare macro on a line)
+            # Backwards compatibility with old versions of the C tools, which
+            # (accidentally) accepted stuff like "--help--" and "-help---".
+            # This was fixed in the C tools by commit c2264564 ("kconfig: warn
+            # of unhandled characters in Kconfig commands"), committed in July
+            # 2015, but it seems people still run Kconfiglib on older kernels.
+            if s.strip(" \t\n-") == "help":
+                return (_T_HELP, None)
+
+            # If the first token is not a keyword (and not a weird help token),
+            # we have a preprocessor variable assignment (or a bare macro on a
+            # line)
             self._parse_assignment(s)
             return (None,)
```

Finally, there's lot of ignoring going on in my grammar in DXR: see https://github.com/mozilla/dxr/blob/master/dxr/query.py#L309, which takes a term like "+foo:bar". It looks at `plus` and `filter`, but it completely ignores `colon`, and the colon propagates no further up the visitation chain.

Turns out the `\Q` failure was due the added back references in comments.  Re chokes on unexpected back references, and in all other cases, we convert them to an appropriate equivalent for what we want to do.  But in comments, we just left them as they were and passed them through.  In past Python versions it didn't matter, but in Python3.6+, it does.

The solution is just to escape them.  Since we are in comments, it doesn't matter if we change their format as they will be stripped out later.

Session management for ChatterBot will essentially just be adding support for having conversations with multiple users at the same time.

Right now, the the `ChatBot` class just stores the current conversation.

```
ChatBot -----> Conversation
```

The change that needs to be made is to allow the chat bot to store multiple conversations.

```
            |-----> Conversation 1
            |
ChatBot --> |-----> Conversation 2
            |
            |-----> Conversation 3
```

Right now my thoughts are as follows, feel free to ask questions or add suggestions.

- Each logic adapter only needs access to the one conversation at a time so no changes to logic adapters should be necessary to facilitate these changes.
- The _current_ conversation will need to be specified by passing an additional argument to the `ChatBot.get_response()` method. This additional argument should be a unique identifier that can be used to select conversation and from the chat bot to be passed to the logic adapter.
- There needs to be a method that adds a new conversation and returns the unique identifier to access it.

**Security:** There is a security component to this. My plan is to investigate how other Python frameworks like Django and Flask handle their session security.

2) Add it without all the formatting edits throughout the script. Although great, it makes it difficult to see what pertains to the utf8 handling, and not sure if any errors might have crept in there. Better as a separate PR.

But only list, dict and set have .copy() methods -- what about int, float, str etc.? I think I meant for the rule to be simple: we always use copy.copy() except when we can prove that that always returns the original object (as a slight optimization and perhaps to avoid having to import the copy module). The special-casing already exists in copy.copy() and copy._copy_dispatch, so we won't have to list it again.

I think I understand part of the reason for the behavior. The ```Editor``` instance initializes the default style for the underlying ```Application```. If I call ```use_colorscheme``` it generates a new ```current_style``` which can be detected and trigger an update of the final styles dict. However if I merely update ```editor.current_style.styles``` this update is not triggered. As shown in the following interactive session in ptpython, where ```e``` is an ```Editor``` instance:

```
>>> e.application.style.get_token_to_attributes_dict()[Token.Toolbar.Status]        
Attrs(color='ffffff', bgcolor='444444', bold=False, underline=False, reverse=False) 
                                                                                    
>>> e.current_style.styles.update({Token.Toolbar.Status : '#ffffff bg:#222222'})    
                                                                                    
>>> e.application.style.get_token_to_attributes_dict()[Token.Toolbar.Status]        
Attrs(color='ffffff', bgcolor='444444', bold=False, underline=False, reverse=False) 
```

However ```use_colorscheme``` does change the dynamic style attributes by overwriting the ```current_style``` attribute:

```
>>> e.application.style.get_token_to_attributes_dict()[Token.Name.Entity]
Attrs(color='999999', bgcolor=None, bold=True, underline=False, reverse=False)

>>> e.use_colorscheme('colorful')

>>> e.application.style.get_token_to_attributes_dict()[Token.Name.Entity]
Attrs(color='880000', bgcolor=None, bold=True, underline=False, reverse=False)
```
So it seems for now I need to either modify ```styles.py``` directly or if I want to overload the behavior I need to emulate the ```use_colorscheme``` method and overwrite ```current_style```.

@tance77, The link I had to the line of code above was the wrong one, really not sure what I was thinking (sorry). [This is the right line in flask-oauthlib/client.py](https://github.com/lepture/flask-oauthlib/blob/391d0f710038669c0d62c3a7287251edd5d47a86/flask_oauthlib/client.py#L465). The other link from the documentation shows an example using Weibo, which coincidentally makes the same kind of mistake Twitch does when supporting the OAuth2 standard, ie they require an 'OAuth' token and not a 'Bearer' token.

Once you have defined `twitch`, you can then provide a `twitch.pre_request` method that will be called to manipulate the request right before it is made. So,


    twitch = oauth.remote_app(
        'twitch',
        consumer_key = 'something',
        ...
    )

    def change_twitch_header(uri, headers, body):
        auth = headers.get('Authorization')
        if auth:
            auth = auth.replace('Bearer', 'OAuth')
            headers['Authorization'] = auth
        return uri, headers, body

    twitch.pre_request = change_twitch_header

You then handle authorization and api calls according as you normally would. You can change uri, headers and body in the `pre_request`. Hope this helps!

These are good questions! I'm happy to help.

1. No. Until the transaction is committed, the data is not visible to other
users. This is something guaranteed by the database engine. There are
database settings that could allow this to happen (it's called a dirty
read, I think), but the defaults disallow it.

2. No. Reversion abuses transactions to preview revisions, but the database
is designed to handle this sort of behaviour. It's  hacky piece of code,
and it makes me feel silly for writing it, but it's also still the best
solution I know of to the problem.

3. No. Worse case, the code generates a bit of disk IO. I can't think of
how this could be abused.

Since you're interested in this, check out the postgresql docs:

https://www.postgresql.org/docs/10/static/transaction-iso.html

Postgresql is a fine piece of software.

I think that @ebertti may have a point in his code, however.

When calling set_db during a revision save, the db specified will be used to save the version and revision models, whereas the rest of the models will be stored in the database specified by their router, or the one specified when save() was called. In other words, the reversion models and the others models may be saved to different databases.

OTOH, the get_deleted method currently assumes that the model and reversion models are in the same database. @ebertti is aiming to decouple this.

My suggestion would be to adapt @ebertti's pull request to add another parameter: model_db, which defaults to db, and specifies the database that contains the model being queried.

At this point, it works as now for single db setup, but supports shared non-default databases and non-shared-non-default databases. In other words, the best of both worlds.

It appears that you hit a funny race condition, followed by a bug that only occurs in Python 2.7.

The race condition was that, somehow, `rest_framework/fonts/glyphicons-halflings-regular.woff` was deleted between django checking that it `exists()`, and then getting it's `modified_time()`.

The Python 2.7 bug is that S3Storage throws an `IOError`, which is not the same as an `OSError` in Python 2.7, so it wasn't caught by collectstatic. The commit I've made changes S3Storage to throw an OSError, which should fix your problem.

I've given this some thought and I actually don't agree that `{"bar": 7.45}` should be a valid json representation of the `Foo` class in your example.

When considering the distinction between:
{"bar": 7.45}
vs.
{"bar": 7.45, "baz": None}

The main problem I see with making the former the json representation of a `Foo` is it would lead to equivalent json representations of two structurally dissimilar types:

```python
class Foo:
    bar: float

class Foo:
    bar: float
    baz: Optional[float]

# these two classes end up having the same json repr
```

However, I'm in favor of adding an option to `from_json` to "infer missing keys as none" (which would be similar to your pr). Technically, this violates a (potential) invariant that `from_json` and `to_json` are inverses, which as nice that may be to provide, supplementing the API to support this usecase seems the more practical approach.

Thus the output of one model (e.g., CoxPH, Cox-Lasso, or many others) is the probability for each patient in each time point. Thus, for each patient, we could get a probability vector that shows the probabilities in each time point of one research period (e.g., 1 month, 2 months, ..., 6 months).  To my limited knowledge of CoxPH, given the features/variables `X` for one patient, the model will compute a `risk` factor by `\beta = Xw`, where `w` is a vector which is learned by minimizing the negative log-likelihood defined by the CoxPH model. For each patient, there is one `\beta`. Thus, for the given set of patients, there are a list of these `\beta`s. Then the authors taken the median of these `\beta`s, and split the set int the `high-risk` group and the `low-risk` group. I'm curious about this. I think the `high-risk` or `low-risk` depends on the patient himself/herself, and should not be from the predicted `\beta`s. I found in many papers, the authors will provide two survival curves or one survival curve as follows:

![a](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/30264480/39664923-0fa6481c-5051-11e8-81cb-424090dcf2bb.PNG)
![b](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/30264480/39664926-1536abe6-5051-11e8-89a2-1320abddc368.png)

I think the bottom figure is easy to understand.

So this was changed from `$("#%(id)s").selectize()` to `$("input[id=#%(id)s]").selectize()` by pull request #8, which to be honest, confuses me. I haven't looked in to it until now, but I did test the previous code with multiple TaggableManager fields in the same model and it worked fine. Each field is going to have a distinct `id`, per how Django sets up the form, but also just due to the rules of HTML. So I don't understand what case would exist where there would be multiple input fields with the same ID, which is what the original PR purports?

So.. let's think through an example. Let's say there is a model like so (Have to use a custom through model to prevent clashes for Taggit)

```
class BlogOtherTags(TaggedItemBase):
    content_object = models.ForeignKey('Blog')

class Blog(models.Model):
    ...
    tags = TaggableManager()
    other_tags = TaggableManager(through=BlogOtherTags, related_name = "blog_other_tags")
```

A ModelForm will correctly generate a form that looks like this:

```
...
<input id="id_other_tags" name="other_tags" type="text" />
...
<input id="id_tags" name="tags" type="text" />
```

And thus, the TagSelectize widget, before PR #8, would generate two sections of JS, one which targets the first field with `$("#id_tags").selectize()` and another which targets the second field with `$("#id_other_tags").selectize()`, thus there's no conflict. Really, all that PR #8 does is change the jQuery selector from targeting any element with the given ID, and instead only targets elements of type `input` which the given ID prepended with '#'. So this patch does indeed fix the problem caused by PR #8, but what I'm getting at, is what is the case that I'm not seeing here that caused the original PR #8?

The code as it is now is broken (you need to append a tuple instead of 2 values). 

Other than that, this looks fine to me. According to [the RFC](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6455) we should return null or nothing in the case the client doesn't specify a subprotocol:
```
/subprotocol/
          Either a single value representing the subprotocol the server
          is ready to use or null.  The value chosen MUST be derived
          from the client's handshake, specifically by selecting one of
          the values from the |Sec-WebSocket-Protocol| field that the
          server is willing to use for this connection (if any).  If the
          client's handshake did not contain such a header field or if
          the server does not agree to any of the client's requested
          subprotocols, the only acceptable value is null.  The absence
          of such a field is equivalent to the null value (meaning that
          if the server does not wish to agree to one of the suggested
          subprotocols, it MUST NOT send back a |Sec-WebSocket-Protocol|
          header field in its response).  The empty string is not the
          same as the null value for these purposes and is not a legal
          value for this field.  The ABNF for the value of this header
          field is (token), where the definitions of constructs and
          rules are as given in [RFC2616].
```

Thanks for taking the time to report here. Hmm, that exception message is confusing, I'm planning to get rid of it at some point. Here, you don't need `get_translation_aware_manager`, that is intended to give translation-aware functionality to a regular, untranslatable model.

The issue you have is `hvad` does not support deep query translation yet. That is, referring to translatable fields of a related model in a query is unsupported (support is planned for hvad2).

I did not test it, but I believe a workaround could be to build the query manually, a bit like this:

```python
r = ModelB.objects.language('pl').filter('tr_model_a__translations__language_code='pl').order_by('tr_model_a__translations__name')
```

I am not sure this will work though. It depends on whether `order_by` will re-use the JOIN generated by `filter` or create a new one. I believe it will but I am not sure so, if you can post the generated query here, I can easily tell you if it is the case or not.

```python 
for item in data:
   try:
       if self.instance is not None:
           id_attr = getattr(self.child.Meta, 'update_lookup_field', 'id')
           # there should also be some preemptive checks
           # that the data actually has the id
           filters = {id_attr: item.get(id_attr, None)}
           self.child.instance = self.instance.filter(**filters).first()
       validated = self.child.run_validation(item)
   except ValidationError as exc:
       errors.append(exc.detail)
   else:
       ret.append(validated)
       errors.append({})
```

Correct me if I am wrong, I don't know what the stance is on rewriting DRF methods.

Hi @likel 

We will eventually add those in, but we don't have concrete plans yet.

Until somewhat recently, some of those endpoints were incorrectly documented, saying that they returned all results, when actually they were paging endpoints. This caused us to accidentally create non-paging methods for some of those. For example, see https://github.com/box/box-python-sdk/blob/master/boxsdk/client/client.py#L231 , which claims to return all groups. When actually, the endpoint is (now) documented as being a paging endpoint (https://docs.box.com/reference#groups).

Box also has plans to add a better paging mechanism to its endpoints. You can see this in some of the newer paging endpoints, for example https://docs.box.com/reference#get-device-pins . Before this becomes available, we don't want to jump the gun and write a method that would be backwards incompatible with the new paging mechanism.

I think we could probably come up with a good mechanism that supports both limit/offset, and the upcoming paging mechanism. And if we do that, then we can implement these methods whenever we want. But we haven't had a chance to sit down and design that yet.

For now, if you need to use those endpoints, you can construct your own calls, with `client.make_request()`.

```python
import mxnet as mx
print(mx.__version__)

from mxnet.gluon.data import DataLoader

import wget

if not os.path.isfile('pickleddata.pkl'):
    wget.download('https://s3.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/tdelteil-test-mxnet/pickleddata.pkl')
data = pd.read_pickle('pickleddata.pkl')


# /!\ The important bit:
NUM_WORKERS = multiprocessing.cpu_count() # number of workers used in the data loading
optimized = True


categories = [
    'Home_and_Kitchen',
    'Books', 
    'CDs_and_Vinyl', 
    'Movies_and_TV', 
    'Cell_Phones_and_Accessories',
    'Sports_and_Outdoors', 
    'Clothing_Shoes_and_Jewelry'
]

ALPHABET = list("abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz0123456789-,;.!?:'\"/\\|_@#$%^&*~`+ =<>()[]{}") # The 69 characters as specified in the paper
ALPHABET_INDEX = {letter: index for index, letter in enumerate(ALPHABET)} # { a: 0, b: 1, etc}
FEATURE_LEN = 1014 # max-length in characters for one document
BATCH_SIZE = 128 # number of documents per batch

def encode(text):
    encoded = np.zeros([len(ALPHABET), FEATURE_LEN], dtype='float32')
    review = text.lower()[:FEATURE_LEN-1:-1]
    i = 0
    for letter in text:
        if i >= FEATURE_LEN:
            break;
        if letter in ALPHABET_INDEX:
            encoded[ALPHABET_INDEX[letter]][i] = 1
        i += 1
    return encoded

class AmazonDataSet(ArrayDataset):
    # We pre-process the documents on the fly
    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        return encode(self._data[0][idx]), self._data[1][idx]


# Data loaders:
split = 0.8
split_index = int(split*len(data))
train_data_X = data['X'][:split_index].as_matrix()
train_data_Y = data['Y'][:split_index].as_matrix()
test_data_X = data['X'][split_index:].as_matrix()
test_data_Y = data['Y'][split_index:].as_matrix()
train_dataset = AmazonDataSet(train_data_X, train_data_Y)
test_dataset = AmazonDataSet(test_data_X, test_data_Y)

train_dataloader = DataLoader(train_dataset, shuffle=True, batch_size=BATCH_SIZE, num_workers=NUM_WORKERS, last_batch='discard')
test_dataloader = DataLoader(test_dataset, shuffle=True, batch_size=BATCH_SIZE, num_workers=NUM_WORKERS, last_batch='discard')

# context:
ctx = mx.gpu() # to run on GPU

# build network
NUM_FILTERS = 256 # number of convolutional filters per convolutional layer
NUM_OUTPUTS = len(categories) # number of classes
FULLY_CONNECTED = 1024 # number of unit in the fully connected dense layer
DROPOUT_RATE = 0.5 # probability of node drop out
LEARNING_RATE = 0.01 # learning rate of the gradient
MOMENTUM = 0.9 # momentum of the gradient
WDECAY = 0.00001 # regularization term to limit size of weights

net = gluon.nn.HybridSequential()
with net.name_scope():
    net.add(gluon.nn.Conv1D(channels=NUM_FILTERS, kernel_size=7, activation='relu'))
    net.add(gluon.nn.MaxPool1D(pool_size=3, strides=3))
    net.add(gluon.nn.Conv1D(channels=NUM_FILTERS, kernel_size=7, activation='relu'))
    net.add(gluon.nn.MaxPool1D(pool_size=3, strides=3))
    net.add(gluon.nn.Conv1D(channels=NUM_FILTERS, kernel_size=3, activation='relu'))
    net.add(gluon.nn.Conv1D(channels=NUM_FILTERS, kernel_size=3, activation='relu'))
    net.add(gluon.nn.Conv1D(channels=NUM_FILTERS, kernel_size=3, activation='relu'))
    net.add(gluon.nn.Conv1D(channels=NUM_FILTERS, kernel_size=3, activation='relu'))
    net.add(gluon.nn.MaxPool1D(pool_size=3, strides=3))
    net.add(gluon.nn.Flatten())
    net.add(gluon.nn.Dense(FULLY_CONNECTED, activation='relu'))
    net.add(gluon.nn.Dropout(DROPOUT_RATE))
    net.add(gluon.nn.Dense(FULLY_CONNECTED, activation='relu'))
    net.add(gluon.nn.Dropout(DROPOUT_RATE))
    net.add(gluon.nn.Dense(NUM_OUTPUTS))


hybridize = True # for speed improvement, compile the network but no in-depth debugging possible

net.collect_params().initialize(mx.init.Xavier(magnitude=2.24), ctx=ctx)

# loss
softmax_cross_entropy = gluon.loss.SoftmaxCrossEntropyLoss()

# optimizer
trainer = gluon.Trainer(net.collect_params(), 'sgd', 
                        {'learning_rate': LEARNING_RATE, 
                         'wd':WDECAY, 
                         'momentum':MOMENTUM})

# Training Loop

import time
start_epoch = 6
number_epochs = 7
smoothing_constant = .01
tick = time.time()
for e in range(start_epoch, number_epochs):
    for i, (review, label) in enumerate(train_dataloader):
        review = review.as_in_context(ctx)
        label = label.as_in_context(ctx)
        with autograd.record():
            output = net(review)
            loss = softmax_cross_entropy(output, label)
        loss.backward()
        trainer.step(review.shape[0])
        
        # moving average of the loss
        if optimized:
            curr_loss = nd.mean(loss)
        else:
            curr_loss = nd.mean(loss).asscalar()
        moving_loss = (curr_loss if (i == 0) 
                       else (1 - smoothing_constant) * moving_loss + (smoothing_constant) * curr_loss)

        if (i%100 == 0):
            tock = time.time()
            if optimized:
                print('Batch {}:{},{},{} seconds for 100 batches'.format(i, curr_loss.asscalar(),moving_loss.asscalar(), tock-tick))
            else:
                print('Batch {}:{},{},{} seconds for 100 batches'.format(i, curr_loss, moving_loss, tick-tock))
            tick = tock

    print("Epoch %s. Loss: %s, Test_acc %s" % (e, moving_loss.asscalar(), test_accuracy))

A lot to unpack here :) Thank you for your help! With so much on the table, I'm thinking that it makes sense to explicitly divide this into phases. The base re-architecture seems like the low hanging fruit we could merge first, allowing independent development of many of these features afterwards. Something like:

### Base re-arch:
- Dag Fetcher abstraction with `BaseDagFetcher`
- Storage location definition (Max called it caching).
- Backwards compatible `FileSystemDagFetcher` with its fetching logic from _dags_folder_ to the cache.
- Dispatching base on URI scheme prefix

It seems like the above could be done in such a way where things would be strictly improved, even before further work.

### Independent features:
- Dag Fetchers for all the things
- Proper unpacking based on suffix (separate `.zip` logic as well as new `.tar.gz`)
- Version semantics
- Security model for dag fetching
- `SETTINGS.DAG_FETCHER`, to support multiple DagFetchers for the same URI scheme
- `SETTINGS.DAG_LIST` callable, to support explicit manifest of which dags to fetch
- Tackle version consistency within a DagRun instance
- Webserver performance when fetching remotely

Just wanted to let you know that I solved my problem! 

**TLDR:** Amazon is trying to be smart with DNS resolving so webhdfs can't access the datanode. Hacking `/etc/hosts` helps.

Longer explanation for future reference:

It has to do with the way Amazon handles DNS resolving.  Each EC2 instance gets an internal IP address and a public IP, and coupled to those are an internal domain name (`ip-xx-xx-xx-xx.eu-west-1.compute.internal`) for the internal IP and an external domain name (`ec2-xx-xx-xx-xx.eu-west-1.compute.amazonaws.com`) for the public IP.

Amazon tries to be smart, so when you use the external domain name on one of your EC2 instances, it will resolve to the _internal_ IP, and when you use that same external domain name outside of Amazon, it will resolve to the public IP.

The problem was that, although I used external domain names when installing the Hadoop cluster, internally those would still resolve to the internal IPs. So when the UDF gets shipped to the cluster, _webhdfs_ will first ask the namenode (for which I provided the external domain name through the impala context) for the applicable datanode to store the blocks, and it will get back an internal IP which resolve to an _internal_ domain name. This cannot be accessed from the outside, so webhdfs cannot save the file to HDFS.

I have sadly not found a way to solve this on the cluster side, so for now I just put the internal domain names in my `/etc/hosts` locally, with the corresponding public IPs. Now I can ship UDFs, and it's awesome!

> This seems magical, and is not at all implied by allow_none=True.

I don't agree with this. It's not magical to have default values for arguments.

I don't see an issue with the current behavior on this. A trait isn't validated until it gets a value (this is deliberate and important). Accessing that value the first time will cause its initialization, which if a value has not been assigned and the default value is not allowed, will raise.

I strongly disagree with this:

> We should probably try to enforce this convention:

> ```python
>  class A(HasTraits):
>      inst = Instance(Foo, default_value=None, allow_none=True)
> ```

That's an extremely verbose signature for a very common, supposedly simple case, especially one that was the default behavior for years. When simple, common things are complicated, the design is probably not right. This is an improvement:

```python
class A(HasTraits):
    inst = Instance(Foo, default_value=None)
```

but ultimately, not an improvement over the current behavior of:

```python
class A(HasTraits):
    inst = Instance(Foo, allow_none=True)
```

We also have to take into account that changing this would break *lots* of existing code throughout IPython, which we simply cannot do here except over multiple major releases and long deprecation cycles.

What I would *really* prefer is to switch Instance back to its original default `allow_none=True`, matching the longstanding behavior of `traits`. Switching to `allow_none=False` by default caused a lot of problems, and doesn't appear to have improved the experience of using traitlets.

The culprit: a param named `scheduler_url` with a value of `None`. It is included in the list of other Luigi system Parameters which are returned by `dir(cls)` in `Task.py` `get_params(cls)` [L210](https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/master/luigi/task.py#L210).

``` python
@classmethod
    def get_params(cls):
        """
        Returns all of the Parameters for this Task.
        """
        # We want to do this here and not at class instantiation, or else there is no room to extend classes dynamically
        params = []
        for param_name in dir(cls):
            param_obj = getattr(cls, param_name)
            if not isinstance(param_obj, Parameter):
                continue

            params.append((param_name, param_obj))

        # The order the parameters are created matters. See Parameter class
        params.sort(key=lambda t: t[1]._counter)
        return params
```

Thanks for submitting this bug report! I'll be taking a look at improving this one today.

The purpose of the count (which comes from the OpenPGP RFC) is to have the hashing process for turning your passphrase into the symmetric key that is used to protect/unprotect your private key take some time so that cracking that passphrase takes longer, and to allow the amount of time taken to be tunable so it can try to keep up with computers as they get faster. (Bearing in mind that I don't actually think that the method used for the current OpenPGP String2Key is very good, but that's what we have to work with)

The unfortunate thing about the count is that very fine adjustments disappear because of the way it's stored in the String2Key specifier (being [expanded](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc4880#section-3.7.1.3) from an 8-bit unsigned integer to a 32-bit unsigned integer), very fine adjustments actually disappear when storing that count.

Given those two things, adding entropy to this process isn't actually going to help at all over just sleeping for a constant time (and sleeping too long might measurably decrease the amount of time taken to run the String2Key process, making your passphrase easier to crack if someone got ahold of your private key)

--

Anyway, the way I wrote that tune_count, it hashes 100 KiB, measures how long that takes, and tries to extrapolate from that measurement how many bytes would need to be hashed for it to take about 100ms. On your system, 100 KiB might not be enough to get a measurable time difference with SHA256 (maybe insufficient resolution in the time source, or your computer is very fast, or something else). At any rate, the robustness of that process could certainly be improved.

One last thing I noticed, while `pool_connection` is the number of conn pools to be cached in memory at any given point (1 conn pool per host), `pool_maxsize` is the total number of connections to keep in a single connection pool.  `pool_maxsize` is what we'd need to accommodate the multithreaded scenario that's been outlined here, but I'm not sure if `pool_connection` matters that much (it would matter if you're accessing multiple AWS services).  In requests, they use the same default value for both:

```

class HTTPAdapter(BaseAdapter):
    def __init__(self, pool_connections=DEFAULT_POOLSIZE,
                 pool_maxsize=DEFAULT_POOLSIZE, max_retries=DEFAULT_RETRIES,
                 pool_block=DEFAULT_POOLBLOCK):
```

I wonder if we should simplify and do something similar, expose a `max_poolsize` and just set that value for both `pool_connection` and `pool_maxsize`.

from splinter import Browser

The ratio is performed on the token set that is partitioned into three parts using both directions of set difference and the intersection of the two sets. 

Three ratios are taken: the intersection over the two set differences, and the ratio of the set differences together. 

The largest of those ratios is returned as the token_set ratio. Hope that helps a little more.

Did some more investigation.  I think Avicii makes for a good worse case example of this since he has songs appearing across many albums.  Currently the two if-continue statements are located after the load is called on the album browser.  One simple optimization is to move these up to the top of of the for loop.  With this approach Avicii takes about 30 seconds or so to load (with no cache) which is imo still too slow, but others may differ.

A couple ideas:
1. Could return to the 1.x style of looping all the tracks for the artist.  This is very fast but doesn't return all the tracks for the artist that may appear on various collections.  I know there is currently a filter for "VARIOUS_ARTIST" however this doesn't filter for instance for the Tron Legacy soundtrack where the artist is listed as Daft Punk but there is a remix by Avicii.  The 1.x style matches well against how I listen to music but may not be good for others.  Or include a configuration option for 1.x style looping.

2. Could keep the 2.x style with the continue optimizations I mentioned above.  I think this is to slow especially since ncmpcpp just hangs without any feedback to the user as to why it is taking so long to load the artist (e.g. a counter showing how how many albums left to load).

3. Could parallelize this.  Currently utils in pyspotify is responsible for loading albums.  It blocks until albums are done loading.  Which means that all albums for an artist are loaded sequentially with blocking IO.  Could create a non-blocking version of load and then a blocking collection construct so that the albums could be loaded as a group (in paralllel) and the blocking only occurs at the group level (i.e. wait until all albums are loaded but don't load them serially).

4. Could asynchronously update the now playing list so that music starts playing right away even though albums are still being loaded.  This is messy if the user decides to clear the now playing list or edit it while tracks are being added plus I don't even know if this is viable.

5. Could add a configuration option to limit the number of albums looped through for an artist since I believe spotify returns the artists primary works and then collections after that.

I personally am most interested in trying the parallel version.  But I thought I'd post these ideas to get some feedback.

It is actually more specific than vcenter allowing certain guests, it is determined by the ESXi host (older versions can't handle newer guest identifiers).

There is an object called the EnvironmentBrowser [1] that is accessible from a ComputeResource (also VirtualMachine, but for determining available guest identifiers, it could be consider a little late?)

Assuming you know the virtual hardware version you want to deal with (or just use the default), you can call
QueryConfigOption(). Pass in the host system if you are on VC.

This returns a LOT of data.

Snippet for getting the guestOs names:
```
env = si.content.rootFolder.childEntity[0].hostFolder.childEntity[0].environmentBrowser
cfgOption = env.QueryConfigOption()
print map(lambda x: x.id, cfgOption.guestOSDescriptor)
```
But I'd also encourage you to check out the rest of the data there and how it can be leveraged for VM provisioning (it gives recommended sizes for the different guest types as well as limit and what is supported).

1: https://www.vmware.com/support/developer/vc-sdk/visdk25pubs/ReferenceGuide/vim.EnvironmentBrowser.html (my googling couldn't find the latest, but this object has been around for a while)

@joakim-hove To continue the discussion that started o Slack, would you mind explaining what's the idea behind the _reference_ object (ie, an instance of `BaseCClass` with `is_reference = True`)? And how is the _parent_ supposed to be used in connection with the reference?

As I interpreted it, a _reference_ object refers to a  C object, but it doesn't own it. So that, when the _refence_ object goes out of scope, it does not free the underlying C object. In practice, this is the equivalent of a non-owning raw pointer in C.

The _parent_ seems to be an arbitrary object that's necessary for the _reference_ object to be valid. My guess is that the _parent_ usually is the python object that owns the underlying C object. In this way, a _reference_ is guaranteed to be valid, and memory is freed only when the last of the references goes out of scope. In practice, this is more or less the equivalent of a `shared_ptr` in C++ 

If my interpretation is correct, then I see why this PR is unwanted

On the other hand, I think letting the _parent_ being an arbitrary object brings in a huge risk of causing circular dependencies

If I interpreted this correctly, I see why this PR shouldn't be merged.
