> For Microsoft Internet Explorer, you may select the appropriate bit version for your workstation (almost certainly the 64-bit version) from the Selenium HQ site.

@GLMeece Are saying the bit-ness or bit version one should choose is based upon the bit version of the OS you are running under? If so that is incorrect. The bit version of the IEDriver depends upon the bit version of IE that one intends to run [as per the SeleniumHQ documentation](https://github.com/SeleniumHQ/selenium/wiki/InternetExplorerDriver). Most versions of Windows have the 32 bit version of IE as the default and most of the time by default people are running 32-bit version of IE. There are several sources [[6](http://answers.microsoft.com/en-us/ie/forum/ie8-windows_7/i-have-windows-7-64-bit-and-need-to-use-internet/babaa5f8-ff06-4ea2-aef6-a9416d65f981)] that talk about 32 bit IE as the default but still looking for ones that back up my claim the 32 bit version has the majority of usage. I best as I can recall the reason for this is all the supporting legacy software and legacy software within the IE ecosystem was for 32 bit IE and thus Microsoft, with their long standing attempt not to break backward compatibility,  never forced users/developers to upgrade and left the 32 bit version as default. Again I am looking for a good source to back this claim up but the majority of people are running 32-bit IE and not 64-bit.

So when you say "appropriate bit version for your workstation" I hear that as saying "the bit version for your operating system" which is incorrect.

A few more resources
1. [A description of the differences between 32-bit versions of Windows Vista and 64-bit versions of Windows Vista ](https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/kb/946765)
2. [Differences between the 32-bit and 64-bit versions of Internet Explorer that are included in the x64-based versions of Windows Server 2003 and in Windows XP Professional x64 Edition](https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/kb/896457)
3. [Version of IE 32 bit or 64 bit? - How can I tell which version I am using?](http://answers.microsoft.com/en-us/ie/forum/ie8-windows_other/version-of-ie-32-bit-or-64-bit-how-can-i-tell/75488de3-fffa-4782-be0a-c536c9a70151)
4. [WebDriver for Internet Explorer (Windows)](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/dn800898%28v=vs.85%29.aspx)
5. [Microsoft Edge: WebDriver](https://developer.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-edge/platform/documentation/dev-guide/tools/webdriver/)
6. [I have Windows 7 64-bit and need to use Internet Explorer 32-bit](http://answers.microsoft.com/en-us/ie/forum/ie8-windows_7/i-have-windows-7-64-bit-and-need-to-use-internet/babaa5f8-ff06-4ea2-aef6-a9416d65f981)

Hey, @emanlove - your assumption about my intent and understanding is not correct. I fully realize one may run both 32-bit as well as 64-bit versions of any executable on Windows. The point of that snippet from my repo's README was that, in general it is considered "best practice" to use a 64-bit executable if you are running a 64-bit OS. Having said that - yes, 32-bit IE almost certainly has the highest deployment/usage. 

If you'll notice the second bullet about IE, I refer to the fact that the 64-bit IE and its correspondent Webdriver has a known bug that causes the _sendKeys_ function to be extremely slow. Thus, I was recommending testing against the 32-bit version of IE, along with the (required) 32-bit version of the Webdriver unless there was a compelling reason to do otherwise.

Bear in mind, what I copied and pasted above is from a larger README, and was originally intended for my company's internal use. If I had taken the time to re-write this for a general audience, I might have taken a more expansive coverage of this topic.

Thanks for your time and concern! :smiley:

This is quite typically problem with web applications which use JavaScript. The element is there but the JavaScript running in the background is not ready. Therefore the interaction with the element does not work.

For me, usually, there are other indicators in the page which can tell is the JavaScript running or not. Sometimes I will have to wait some other element or an attribute of am element (same or other) and then perform the desired action. If there are not obvious indicators, I go and discuss with the developer about the problem and usually we can find a suitable solution for the problem in hand. For the small amount of problems, I am forced to use sleep, Wait Until Keyword Succeeds or both. Example like this:
```| Click Login Button |
| | Wait Until Keyword Succeeds | 10 | 2 | Helper Click |

| Helper Click |
| | Clock Element | xpath=//button/xpath/here |
| | Wait Until Page Contains Element | xpath=//next/page/xpath/here | 1s | ```

Note: The above code is untested and written out of my memory, so it may contain errors.. 

Also these kinds of ugly helpers should be last tool in your arsenal and not the first one. Because perhaps it's a real bug and there might be better solution to your problem.

But as bottom line, this looks like it is not a Selenium2Library issue, instead it looks your web application problem. At least for me it strongly looks that way, but I can't not be sure. If you disagree, more details from your problem is needed.

I was able to recreate this issue with `pipenv-resolver pandas-datareader==0.5.0 pandas==0.18.1", using:
```
$ pipenv --version
pipenv, version 11.6.1
```

I believe the source of the dependency conflict is in `pipenv.patched.notpip.index.PackageFinder.find_requirement`, with the value of `ignore_compatiblity=True`. (Which was recently changed with this patch, https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/commit/4e906e4bc0df095ed3d61d4d283af02c76ec76d2#diff-6793f5b689d9956dc9b6c175038b718dR491)

When `ignore_compatibility` is `True`, and the spec for `pandas-datareader` is `pandas-datareader==0.5.0`, the spec is ignored, and all available candidates that are listed on PyPI are considered. The `best_candidate` value in the `find_requirements` method is then set to `0.6.0`, instead of `0.5.0`, which adds a constraint of `pandas>=0.19.2`. The correct pandas constraint for `pandas-datareader==0.5.0` is `pandas>=0.17.0`.

Here is a table of the expected constraints for `pandas-datareader` with links to the project's `setup.py`:

| pandas-datareader version | pandas contsraint | setup.py link |
|-------------------------------------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| `0.5.0`                                 | `>=0.17.0`             | https://github.com/pydata/pandas-datareader/blob/331e3895c51326e87e69325e1a14f1e350cb832e/setup.py#L28 |
| `0.6.0`                                 | `>=0.19.0`            |  https://github.com/pydata/pandas-datareader/blob/0debb023ba90ec57b6dd134a2003cb07aeea66a4/setup.py#L17 |

The following patch, which sets the default value of `find_requirements` to `False`, allows a successful invocation of the `pipenv-resolver pandas-datareader==0.5.0 pandas==0.18.1`. (Clearing the depcache before re-running the command is also required.)

```
modified   pipenv/patched/notpip/index.py
@@ -488,7 +488,7 @@ class PackageFinder(object):
             dependency_versions
         )
 
-    def find_requirement(self, req, upgrade, ignore_compatibility=True):
+    def find_requirement(self, req, upgrade, ignore_compatibility=False):
         """Try to find a Link matching req
 
         Expects req, an InstallRequirement and upgrade, a boolean
```

I am not yet sure if changing that default value will break any other use cases, or cause a regression on https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/1667, the issue which is mentioned in the patch that added the `ignore_compatibility` flag.

> There problem here seems to be that at some point the `pkg_resources.extern.pyparsing` module is just named `pyparsing` and then gets renamed at some point.

That seems incorrect to me. Throughout the `pkg_resources` code, only `pkg_resources.extern.pyparsing` is imported. Can you demonstrate what you mean or explain it in another way?

> Both the modules pyparsing and pkg_resources.extern.pyparsing are later 2.1.5.

The way the vendoring code works is it prefers the vendored version if available but will use the canonical version if the vendored version is not available and it installs this resolved package into the `extern` namespace. So I would expect on a system where `pkg_resources._vendor.pyparsing` is missing or broken, to resolve `pkg_resources.extern.pyparsing` as the root `pyparsing` (i.e. 2.1.5).

> Looking at sys.module I can see that I have two pyparsing modules.

This is expected. Due to the preference for the vendored package, a preference which @dstufft said was preferable because it meant that pkg_resources could more reliably depend on a specific version, there would have to be two modules.

> Now my tests were failing because the ParseException was never actually caught.

Can you confirm that the uncaught ParseExceptions are coming from calls in `pkg_resources`? If so, you won't be able to rely on `pyparsing.ParseException` to catch those, but you'll need to catch the exception from the `extern` namespace (`pkg_resources.extern.pyparsing.ParseException`).

One thing I'd caution that @dstufft didn't explicitly mention in his initial post is to clearly separate the "rewrite to fix the fundamental architectural issues" step (where the focus should be on minimising regressions relative to the previous code base) from the "implement or fix features that were infeasible under the old architecture" step (which would be better handled through additional issues that are blocked by this one). Scope creep on rewrites (aka "second system syndrome") is one of their leading causes of failure. The parallel release and maintenance strategy is quite effective at dealing with that problem, which is one of the reasons I suggested it on the previous PR.

One other general motivation for a rewrite potentially worth mentioning is that the bootstrapping situation has changed significantly since virtualenv was first created - pip is now provided by default with both Python 2 & Python 3, avoiding the previous question as to whether virtualenv should be used to bootstrap pip or vice-versa.

> For components that don't need configuration, does SimpleSpout().spec(parallelism_hint=2 also work over SimpleSpout.spec(parallelism_hint=2)?

`SimpleSpout()` would instantiate the spout, which isn't something we really want people doing, since that might run code that doesn't make sense outside of the topology (like things that connect to a DB, etc.).



> I see that the java bolt takes another bolt (not a stream) as its input. Are these interchangeable?

`inputs` is always either a list of components (i.e., bolts and spouts), or a dict that maps from components to groupings.



> Is args_list also available to ShellBolt?

No, because `ShellBolt` and `JavaBolt` are just thin wrappers around the underlying Thrift classes that Storm uses for serializing the topology spec.  `ShellBolt` just takes a command and a script (although script can actually be a space delimiter set of arguments), and `JavaBolt.arg_list` is the list of arguments that are given to the constructor in Java.

The exact meaning of `'default'` background is unspecified by ECMA-48 (see 49 [here](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANSI_escape_code#graphics)). So different terminals might implement it differently. 

I think iTerm simply substitutes `'default'` for the background colour from its color theme, while your implementation treats it as white.

`pyte` only aims to support `TERM=linux` for now with some minor extensions pulled from `xterm` (like colour support). Therefore, it is recommended to use `TERM=linux` for running apps inside `pyte`.

Ideally, we should not handle `how > 3` at all, and just render the CSI sequence as-is. It is probably best to do this directly in `Screen` without complicating the already complex parser in `Stream`.

This might have utility in a few different places.  The code is remarkably concise; we could place it in a hidden file and reference where needed.  The first use that comes to my mind would be to add a new type of noise option ('blue'?) to skimage.util.random_noise.

In general, this is a pattern that goes several places in the viewer API, which makes it hard to find the program flow. In example, plugins [append themselves](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/master/skimage/viewer/plugins/base.py#L122) to the viewer, though the viewer [calls attach](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/master/skimage/viewer/viewers/core.py#L249).

To me, it seems more clean to have the code modifying the object inside the same object. That way, reading the source for one class will give you a fairly good overview of all the internals of the class.

@lnielsen I see your point, but I think you fix it at the wrong place. IMHO it's just not working to derive document types from the existing data correctly. 

I look at it the other way around. You get a user submission. (This is the same here. No cataloguers no developers. Joe Doe is submitting.) Now, if I as a user select "journal article" as document type, I, as a user, would expect that the record in question is formatted as `@article` in bibtex and not as `@misc` simply cause I did not add the volume yet. (Probably, it's "ahead of print" stuff.) Sure, bibtex will issue a warning (no error, btw.) but well, this is to be expected and it is even ok. I get a journal reference with a missing field. But better than getting no journal reference at all as in `@misc`...

IMHO this holds for all other document types as well.

> You should have as much metadata as possible and check and fix problems in it, agreed in principle. However, it can be a very expensive in terms of cataloging/developer man-power and depends on use cases.

I do _not_ think about manual curation after submission. 

Usually, you have to rely on what the users submits. Perfectly agree. Also agreed that this might be crap. Still, I would expect the submission interface that it tells me as the user what fields would be necessary for the document type chosen to be a complete reference. Ie. that an article submission signifies that you have to add the journal. So make it bold an red or whatever.

It's an _other_ question whether it just marks the field or whether you're enforcing it's input and completely unrelated to whether you have manpower for curation down the road. Depending on your installation you might choose either one. Again perfectly agreed. At join2 actually we have both: for internal records we signify that journal is a "must have" for articles but we don't enforce it. For records that get some upstream formal curation and end up in the bean counting of our instiutions you have to add the journal and we don't accept the record for submission unless you do so. Still both records would get formatted as `@article` as the user chose them to be journal articles. Ie. the bioprocessor on layer 8 told us to do so.

In your sense bibtex export in the first case would be broken cause it issues a warning. I take this as a feature: it tells me, probably much later, that I missed to add the journal. Formatting as `@misc` will not notify me and I'll have to scan and compare my bibliography manually and check in each case if it shouldn't have a journal entry. 

I think this very same logic holds for all document types, as all that the document type signifies is the list of mandatory fields. Mandatory in the sense of the ability to produce a correct citation while correct itself is defined as "this item is retrievable and the display follows conventions for this". Ok, one could drop all the text clutter and just cite the DOI right away, would save a lot of space, perfectly agreed. Just doesn't yet follow the conventions.

Thus I think the bibtex export like all other bibliographic exports is not so dependent on your instance or use case.

Building a static .a library would be helpful for integrating Brotli into projects.  I've been developing a backup program, HashBackup, and everything is loaded statically into one self-contained executable.

Brotli is a great compression program, but is complex compared to zstd.  I have to build on old platforms to have upward compatiblity, so that's:

- Snow Leopard (OSX 10.6.8) on Mac
- Centos 5 for Linux
- FreeBSD 7

These seem ancient, but I have enterprise-y customers still using them.  Building zstd into Python/HashBackup was easy.  Adding Brotli was not so easy:
- it doesn't compile on FreeBSD 7 - I guess gcc is too old
- earlier versions worked on OSX, but yesterday's version expands instead of compressing (separate issue)
- the version I downloaded yesterday doesn't build on OSX 10.6.8 with make

A believe a couple actions should be taken:
 - Upon receiving a too large entity, the server should log a proxy exception, alerting the user that an event happened which was un-capturable.  This would prevent silent doom.
 - An API should be devised to all the automatic or selective truncation of large strings when errors are thrown.  E.g., if I have an attribute error on a dictionary with a large string key, in many cases I don't have any need to know the contents of that key, beyond perhaps the first 100 chars to indicate its further contents.

Thanks very much for this explanation.  I think I understand what you are getting at much better now, and in fact it's something that comes up for HSC as well.  Our data are deep enough that this flux limit in the catalog means some galaxies are not represented, so we have to either (a) extrapolate the catalog to represent all galaxies past our flux limit or (b) do science with a brighter subset of galaxies that are well-represented by this catalog (and that can be obtained by simulating with this catalog, then applying cuts).  Ultimately, I think the answer is that COSMOS is not a sufficient parent catalog to use on its own for simulations for DES or HSC; we need a deeper HST dataset for that purpose, or we have to do some kind of clever tricks on COSMOS.

So, I do see a use case for what you are suggesting.  I don't have a great answer for how to do it with the existing catalog.  If you have suggestions for how to achieve this, I would be happy to help make it easier to do what you want using GalSim (e.g., a setting in COSMOSCatalog() constructor or whatever).  I should note that in #808, the idea is to define the joint distribution of Sersic parameters using the part of the catalog that isn't too close to the flux limit, so as to avoid the realm where selection effects are important.  Then we use those joint distributions for the extrapolation, so I think that the extrapolated catalog might be just what you are requesting?

I now realized that I had made changes in this function to make it faster but I forgot to do a pull request! @juanchopanza I  put here the code, in case you would like to use it. I use masks instead of resizing lists:

``` python
    @staticmethod
    def remove_duplicate_points(points, groups):
        ''' Removes the duplicate points from the beginning of a section,
        if they are present in points-groups representation.

        Returns:
            points, groups with unique points.

        '''

        def _find_last_point(group_id, groups):
            ''' Identifies and returns the id of the last point of a group'''
            group_initial_ids = np.sort(np.transpose(groups)[0])

            if group_id != len(group_initial_ids) - 1:
                return group_initial_ids[np.where(group_initial_ids ==
                                                  groups[group_id][0])[0][0] + 1] - 1

        to_be_reduced = np.zeros(len(groups))

        # mask of the points that will be returned. Starts as true, i.e. all points
        # will be returned and if a duplicate is found, the respective index will
        # become false
        p_mask = np.ones(len(points), dtype=np.bool)

        for ig, g in enumerate(groups):
            if g[2] != -1 and np.allclose(points[g[0]],
                                          points[_find_last_point(g[2], groups)]):
                # Set duplicate's index to False in order to remove
                # it from the resulting array
                p_mask[g[0]] = False
                # Reduce the id of the following sections
                # in groups structure by one
                to_be_reduced[ig + 1: len(groups)] += 1

        groups = np.array([np.subtract(i, [j, 0, 0])
                           for i, j in itertools.izip(groups, to_be_reduced)])

        return points[p_mask], groups
```

I had the same issue if you are running a https server with apache and have set:

Cache-Control: no-store

you need to remove it.

if for security reasons you still need to set no-store you can add this rule to the server apache conf:

<FilesMatch "\.(ttf|otf|eot|woff)$">
  <IfModule mod_headers.c>
    Header set Access-Control-Allow-Origin "*"
    Header set Cache-Control "max-age=3600"
  </IfModule>
</FilesMatch>

basically you are going to allow IE to store the cache with the font files for 1 hour. which wont affect the security.

Hi, @liorwohl. Thanks for the extra info and helping us get this resolved. 

We circled our wagons as a team and our current plan is to keep the current version of `comment-alt` the way it is since it fits the new visual and naming convention standards we moved to with version 5. Its a visual alternative to the `comment` icon.

Now we totally recognize (thanks to you) that V4's `comment-alt` is a different icon than this one. It has always conveyed different information than `comment`. And to move it to our current standards and expand the content of comment/chat icons like we've done, we needed to rename that visual icon to `comment-dots`. 

We totally get how that messes with a look and feel of a project you're using FA on now. So we're going to do what we've done with other V4 icons that have visually changed but that our users have depended on in their existing projects, including:

 1. adding the regular style of `comment-dots` into Font Awesome Free for everyone to use
 2. mapping `fa fa-comment-alt` to translate to `far fa-comment-dots` for users of our Version 4 Shim file](https://fontawesome.com/how-to-use/upgrading-from-4#v4-shim).

We're getting these changes ready now. Hope that helps you keep using the icon you want while moving to Font Awesome 5's content and standards. 😄

yeah, this is a pretty clear shortcoming of the tool. It's a very reasonable request to do some simple aliasing of the inventory source name.

I'd still like to keep the query as specific as possible. Unfortunately, I think the pk (59 in that case) is specific to the inventory _source_, meaning that there's no way to predict the real name before actually grabbing it. The best I could suggest is to use a querystring that matches `name__startswith=aws (aws-inventory - ` in cases like these.

From the user perspective, I would suggest that we require yaml-file syntax like

```yaml
inventory_source: aws (aws-inventory - x)
```

in order to flag that it should be looked up via this more lenient method.

It would be possible to create a uniqueness problem in the specific case where you created 2 organizations with an inventory & group of the same name. That might be worth a mention in the docs, but nothing more.

Personally, I am not in favor of using libraries in another language than Python (it is hard to maintain mes-aides-ui with NodeJs and Python).

## Performance-wise

Round trip check should be limited to files modified from either `origin/master` or the pull request base. Performance would than be proportional to the number of YAML file changed in the branch.

We can try to improve [ruamel.yaml](https://pypi.org/project/ruamel.yaml/).

## Workflow-wise

I agree it is not optimal and might be frustrating, linting/_round-triping_ should be more discoverable. However, the painful workflow you are describing is quite common for lint constraints.

On of the goals of that PR is to limit changes in YAML files to facilitate reviews and limit Git history (after manual or systematic editions).

We can try to make [ruamel.yaml](https://pypi.org/project/ruamel.yaml/) a Python equivalent of Prettier.

Je commence une liste des unités manquantes pour la France:
 - mois, jours, heures -> month, day, hour
 - date (année) -> date
 - nombre de parts fiscales -> custom pour France 
 - nombre d'individus/enfants

En effet les erreurs `Circular definition...` sont plus complexes et le mécanisme de gestion des cycles n'est encore pas documenté à ma connaissance.

J'ai compris à présent que tu travaille sur ta machine et que tu peux copier le notebook sur le serveur afin d'échanger. Je te demandais cela afin que tu ne sois pas dépendant de notre rythme de mises à jour du serveur. Le code écrit dans les notebooks correspond implicitement à une certaine version de France.

En somme cela soulève de notre côté le besoin de mettre à jour le serveur régulièrement pour les paquets Core et France, pour régler cette question.

Premièrement je vais mettre à jour le serveur manuellement puis nous pourrons éventuellement envisager une automatisation. Ceci pourra déclencher de nouvelles erreurs, mais le notebook tournera sur une base plus à jour.

Actuellement en séminaire d'équipe ainsi que demain, je ne peux m'engager à traiter cela rapidement mais je tenais à te faire une réponse tout de même.

Il est vrai que je suis plus intéressé par le calcul de l’IRPP et de ses
composantes (nombre de parts, réductions d’impôt, crédits d’impôts,
plafonnements, contributions sociales sur les revenus du capital, etc…),
mais je trouvais intéressant également de pouvoir déterminer le salaire de
base à partir du salaire imposable.

Mais concernant ce dernier point et comme tu le dis, cela ne peut  être
qu’approximatif : un indépendant va cotiser à la RSI, un salarié d’une
entreprise à la sécurité sociale (les taux ne sont pas les mêmes), un
dirigeant ne cotise pas pour les ASSEDIC etc… Donc, plein de cas différents…

Le résultat du test_case est donc souvent faux ou approximatif. Il va
falloir que mon application utilise plutôt l’API calculate que l’API
simulate.

Le calcul de l’IRPP est faux aussi, par exemple : tu saisis un montant de
50000 € dans pensions alimentaires perçues (1AO), le montant de l’impôt est
de 8254 € alors qu’il devrait être de 8249 € . Et il en résulte dans le
démonstrateur des aides de fin d’année (152 €) et du RSA(6167 €)  et donc un
revenu disponible de 48065 €, ce qui ne correspond pas à la réalité. Je ne
pense pas qu’une personne qui perçoit 50000 € de pensions alimentaires
puisse bénéficier de ces aides.

Autre exemple avec 50000 € pour salaire de base : Il en résulte un salaire
imposable de 44490 €. Et un impôt de 5592 €. Alors qu’un salaire imposable
de 44490 € devrait donner un impôt de 7708 €.

Il me semble que tu n'en auras pas besoin car si tu souhaites calculer
uniquement l'IRPP <http://legislation.openfisca.fr/variables/irpp>  (peux-tu
confirmer ?) alors il te suffit de demander à l'utilisateur son salaire
imposable <http://legislation.openfisca.fr/variables/salaire_imposable> .

La réforme d'inversion de revenus permet de passer du salaire imposable au
salaire de base, mais de façon approchée. Y a-t-il une autre raison qui te
pousserait à vouloir remonter au salaire de base ? Ou bien souhaites-tu
calculer par exemple des charges sociales ?

Le fichier datatrees.py
<https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/blob/a3772aef8b5e58876205b6c0
bf30b1fcb888c06b/openfisca_france/model/datatrees.py#L37> , qui stocke les
catégories dans lesquelles sont rangées les variables d'entrée, connaît
uniquement les variables d'entrée. Et la vraie variable d'entrée du point de
vue du système socio-fiscal, c'est le salaire de base. Les autres salaires
sont calculés, mais comme tu le sais on peut les court-circuiter en leur
fournissant une valeur, auquel cas le moteur ne les calcule pas.

Je distingue donc 2 cas :
* 
* Soit tu souhaites seulement calculer l'IRPP, dans ce cas tu changes à
chaud la variable salaire_de_base en salaire_imposable, après avoir
téléchargé le JSON depuis ton application.
* 
* Ou alors tu souhaites calculer des charges sociales et dans ce cas, si tu
veux, tu actives la réforme d'inversion de revenus comme documenté ici
<https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-web-api/blob/next/docs/reforms.md#te
st-if-it-works> . Tu devras aussi modifier à chaud la variable
salaire_de_base en salaire_imposable du fait de l'utilisation de la réforme.

Est-ce plus clair ? Je m'attaque aux issues créées pour l'occasion.

> What about something more explicit where you can give it a list of buckets to configure?

Totally - just having stable Chalice with S3 events would be awesome. For now I forked Chalice into our organization and merged the PR to use it for PoC development.

> I would be in favor of making it an explicit list as well.

I understand the concern. We definitely do not need to create S3 buckets from the app, but we have a use case which will benefit from wildcards a lot. We have list of clients and some of them (not everyone!) are getting their personal S3 bucket to share some content with our system. We need Chalice to parse every new uploaded file for each client and we do not know the list of buckets ahead of time - we just have the bucket naming convention. Having explicit list of buckets will require us to re-deploy Chalice app every time new bucket is created - and we are expecting more and more clients using this feature. So having a wildcard will reduce amount of deployments dramatically.

Looking at Rasa it has 116M of just pure dependencies. Lambda has a package size limit of 50MB [currently](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/limits.html) so rasa won't be deployable to Lambda regardless of Chalice packaging it or not.

That being said it does appear that there is an issue with packaging Twisted here. The extension is `tar.bz2` which is technically not a valid packaging extension given PEP 527, though it can be made to work. So I will mark this as a feature request specifically for `bz2` support for packaging in general, rasa still won't work though since its far too large of a dependency tree.

This may not be the right place to bring up this particular `makeotf` bug related to the 'aalt' GSUB feature, but if a referenced GSUB feature (of the form `feature xxxx;`) references its own explicit lookups (of the form `lookup xxxxxxxxxx;`), the substitutions in such lookups, even if they are of the allowed type (such as single substitutions), are ignored.

The 'aalt' GSUB feature of the [Source Han Sans](https://github.com/adobe-fonts/source-han-sans) family does not include the substitutions from the somewhat large (in terms of the number of substitutions) 'locl' GSUB feature, which uses only explicit lookups to support language-specific substitutions. (The current `features` files no longer reference the 'locl' GSUB feature in the 'aalt' GSUB feature for this reason.)

Use an `appengine_config.py` like so:

```python
"""
`appengine_config.py` is automatically loaded when Google App Engine
starts a new instance of your application. This runs before any
WSGI applications specified in app.yaml are loaded.
"""

import sys
from google.appengine.ext import vendor

# Third-party libraries are stored in "lib", vendoring will make
# sure that they are importable by the application.
vendor.add('lib')

def unload_module(module_name):
    target_modules = [m for m in sys.modules if m.startswith(module_name)]
    for m in target_modules:
        if m in sys.modules:
            del sys.modules[m]

unload_module('oauth2client')
unload_module('pyasn1_modules')
```

Make sure you have this at the top of your API-defining modules (e.g., the scripts referenced by `app.yaml`):

```python
try:
    appengine_config
except NameError:
    import appengine_config
```

Yes, the w3c spec support composite foreign keys. Generally, I think, it is best to not regard "foreign key" as some sort of column type, but follow the "constraint" paradigm: i.e. "foreign keys" is a list of constraints on the values of a row, rather than a core property of the individual columns. Thus it shouldn't interfere with writing at all,  but will come into play only when calling a dataset's `validate` method.

class WorkerThread(threading.Thread):

> So the current situation is that we're using urandom-seeded Mersenne Twister, right?

I'd expect MT to be seeded from real entropy, yes. We use the global one (from stdlib).

> I think "B" above (fixed all-0's mask) might be non-spec-compliant?

From the letter of the spec, yes. From the intention of the spec, no. Practically (and that means, running over TLS), it just burns CPU additional cycles without gain.

"no" because the bytes on the wire for a TLS connection cannot be selected from app code using a TLS implementation, and presumably broken intermediaries hence cannot be deliberately spooked.

MITM proxies which can unwrap TLS, but then either are broken themselfes (dont understand WS) or send bytes downstream over a non-TLS and broken intermediary: yes, right. I'm out;)

Note that AB (deliberately) deviates from the spec already: we fail by hard dropping a connection upon protocol violation without performing a closing handshake by default (you can override that). Trying to continue talking to a broken client increases the number of things that can go wrong down the line.

Sidenote: the IETF WG spent many many hours fighting about masking - and the HTTP/2 WG again (they don't have any masking btw!). With "this is snake oil" up to "it's not enough: we need to reinvent full encryption" opinions. This is the "rough consensus" part of the IETF at work;)

This is actually a wider issue than just ApplicationRunner as there is really no callback to hang onto for any use-case where many parts of the handshake fail. Conceptually, part of the statemachine looks like this:

```
tcp connection (successful) -> attempt handshake -> handshake success (create session, call onConnect())
```
So if anything goes wrong on the TCP connection itself, that bubbles out as an errback/exception to the original ``.connect()`` call (looking at the Twisted case). So, that's fine. But, if the tcp connection works (e.g. you're connecting to a "normal" webserver) but the handshake fails (because it doesn't speak websocket at all, for example, or the TLS fails) then there's no errback (in fact, the Deferred from the connect() has allready callback()'d anyway) and there isn't even an ApplicationSession object created yet, so we can't even call ``onDisconnect`` on it ...

I'm adding LTS API as we should ensure these use-cases work properly with that.I believe the Connection API we'd discussed/prototyped in the new-api discussions would be able to handle this cleanly, as any failure at all before getting to 'handshake success' would/could just be an errback.

Most projects have a single Dockerfile at the root of the project.  AllenNLP is a bit unusual in that we have two--but I think that it makes sense as we have two separate roots for GPU and CPU support.  We could in theory build the GPU image from the CPU image, but that would take some serious work to re-implement GPU support and it might take a long time to build the GPU image.

I think it is very unfortunate if we need to add a third Dockerfile, which is essentially a copy of `Dockerfile.gpu`.  It makes it even harder to change our Dockerfiles, because now most changes need to be made in three places.

If there is no mounting/downloading solution then I would suggest building `Dockerfile.gpu.train` from `Dockerfile.gpu`.  Here is some pseudocode about how your script would change.

```
docker build -f Dockerfile.gpu -t allennlp-gpu
docker build -f Dockerfile.gpu.train -t allennlp-gpu-train # this will extend the previous step
```

@krancour we need to document this if it isn't documented yet. I agree that etcd2 behaves as expected. Before deis 1.9.0, we were using etcd 0.4. New nodes replaced by the autoscaling group joins the cluster as members since etcd 0.4 didn't have proxies then.

With deis 1.9.0 and etcd2, the behavior changed. So with deis 1.9.0 and above, you have to promote proxies to members. Otherwise, when 2 nodes are replaced (on a 3-node cluster), etcd loses its quorum.

Currently, by using autoscaling group on AWS, my assumption (and probably most people too) is etcd can recover on its own. We probably just need to add a warning that it's not the case anymore starting deis 1.9.0.

I agree on using a separate etcd cluster. That increases the minimum number of servers needed but might be the best option in production. This is for a different ticket though.

There should be some docs, for sure, but I can share my experience for some anecdotal stories.  The first thing to note should be, for most of my experience I have been running the minimum number of nodes with the minimum amount of RAM (4GB).

I have heard it said that this is not enough RAM for ceph to correctly recover from an unexpected store node going out.  So, your experience may vary with adding more RAM on each node.

I have also heard it said that Deis requires a minimum of 3 nodes to operate normally.  This seems to be almost correct from my experience -- the caveat that if you have 3 nodes and your apps are all scaled to 2 or more workers, you may be able to suffer the loss of one node without incurring any downtime on your apps, but you won't be able to deploy new apps or app versions until you relieve the situation.

With only two nodes, even with enough RAM, I suspect you will probably get split-brained.  You need three or more to be up for the usual reason in any cluster, even numbers during leader election can result in ties.

For me, this "relieving the situation" has usually meant rebuilding the cluster.  (It's significantly faster than diagnosing ceph issues or attempting a recovery to just rebuild the whole thing from scratch.  This might not be true if I knew more about ceph.  That stuff is probably very well documented.)

Advice I have received says that building 5-node clusters will leave you resilient to the loss of 2 nodes.  I have not confirmed this experimentally; the only time I have "tested" removing nodes (oops), it was 3 nodes of 5 going down at once, my apps were not scaled up to run on each node, the two machines that were left were less than a quorum, therefore the failure did not result in apps gracefully remaining available to the router, and ceph did not come back so fleet could not restore the downed containers successfully.

A doc detailing "the various ways that DEIS can fail (both gracefully and otherwise)" might be best approached with a format like "Don't do what Donny Don't does."  The summary at the top could probably read "have enough nodes with enough ram and don't forget to scale your apps."

Any other advice?  "Try it in a test environment before it happens in production" is one more.

This is what I got from [Docker's documentation](https://docs.docker.com/engine/userguide/networking/default_network/configure-dns/):

> Regarding DNS settings, in the absence of the --dns=IP_ADDRESS..., --dns-search=DOMAIN..., or --dns-opt=OPTION... options, Docker makes each container’s /etc/resolv.conf look like the /etc/resolv.conf of the host machine (where the docker daemon runs). When creating the container’s /etc/resolv.conf, the daemon filters out all localhost IP address nameserver entries from the host’s original file

So for v1, I know we don't supply any way to add any additional flags to the `docker run` operation. You will need to modify your host's resolv.conf in order for changes to occur within the container, or create a patch for Deis to plug into the container's dns resolver with an option flag in the fleet manifest file.

I'm OK with the approach overall, but I don't love the class-based interface.

Aside from `__init__`, the class has three public methods, of which only one is actually called publicly (`print_response`). Generally, callers are instantiating the class with some arguments, calling `print_response()` on the new instance with some other arguments, and then discarding it. The `commands.endpoint.is_activated` module is an exception that actually saves the instance and then calls `print_response()` in different ways conditionally.

As a caller to this, I think I would prefer just having `globus_cli.safeio` expose a single callable that combines the arguments that go to `__init__` with those that go to `print_response` (so I don't have to remember which ones go where) along with the various constants (e.g. `FORMAT_TEXT_TABLE`).

As you can see by #7173 and back to #448, this is a contentious, long-standing issue.  There's no clear agreement on how to solve it.  The issue is complicated by the fact that this really isn't a conda thing at all; it's all part of the python interpreter.  Of course we can actively patch this behavior out of the python interpreter in the python that conda installs, but changing behavior like that to something we don't "own" is always incredibly controversial.

For your use case right now, I would recommend setting the `PYTHONNOUSERSITE` environment variable.

My thoughts on where we're headed in general on this topic... For activated non-`base` conda environments, `conda activate` will actually go ahead and set the `PYTHONNOUSERSITE` environment variable, I'm suggesting you do now yourself.  By default it will not set it for the `base` environment, whether the base environment is properly activated or not.  This behavior will all be configurable, and there appear to be three states to what we're proposing for python interpreter isolation from `~/.local`: `disabled`, `non-base`, `all`.  The default value for the configuration, as proposed, would be the `non-base` one.

Whilst the main problem is the shuffling of gflags/libpng with the `update zlib` command (possibly due to features) the `update all` also demonstrates undesirable behaviour (IMHO) which I'll detail below.

```
(shuffle) λ conda update --all
...
    gflags: 2.2.1-vc14_0  conda-forge [vc14] --> 2.2.1-h6538335_0
    libpng: 1.6.34-vc14_0 conda-forge [vc14] --> 1.6.34-h79bbb47_0
```
> *^-- notice that we have exactly the case you want, the packages from the lower priority channel get replaced by the ones from the higher priority one.*

This statement is incorrect - the above behaviour is exactly what I do not want!!

By specifying `channel_priority=false` I explicitly do not want the channel order to play any part in the selection of a package to install. The obvious question this raises is then how does the solver choose between two perfectly valid install plans - one with the `conda-forge` and the other with the `defaults` package?

My idea here is to apply a *"minimum shuffling"* constraint - i.e choose the plan which will minimise any changes to the existing environment. In this case that means that if one of the `defaults` or `conda-forge` packages is already installed and both are valid install plans then the plan with the already installed package will win. So in the specific `update all` example above, because the `conda-forge` packages are already installed the plan keeping the already installed packages (and making no changes) would win.

By specifying `channel_priority=false` I only want a package to change if there is a newer version available. Build strings, features or channel ordering shouldn't play a part (in so far as that is possible)

My thoughts on how to implement this are as briefly outlined in https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/6806#issuecomment-361151561 - i.e. include an `is_already_installed` flag so that the ordering would be:
```
(version_number, is_already_installed, channel_priority)
```
In this way `channel_priority` would only ever come into play when installing a new package such that the `is_already_installed` flag is False for all install plans.

I think this PR is the way to go. The "minimize packages to be removed" sounds like a good idea and I would have bought it "on paper", but in use it has been very confusing. 

If the user is not satisfied with an additional removal due to dependencies, they can manually decide how to proceed, e.g. not remove anything, downgrade or whatever. 

If you wish conda to keep some of the current behavior (which admittedly can be useful in some circumstances), you may make it spit an additional message about the possible downgrade, so the user doesn't have to check it themselves. In the case of your example, the message could say "removing blaze version such-and-such because it requires numba, you may install older blaze version older-such-and-such which doesn't". In my opinion, the `conda remove` does not need to do the full dependency analysis for this message, it's enough to check just if an older version of the removed packages lacks the dependency of the asked-to-be-removed package. If the user care, they will run `conda install` and the full dependency analysis will be run there.

> I think I'm misunderstanding something. Why is rebuilding the package with build_number + 1 not an option?

We have been through this many times internally, I'm afraid, suggesting that it needs to be documented.

The problem is that if you leave the old package in place, then it's possible for `conda` to still pick up that broken package with specific combinations of dependencies.

For instance, suppose package `foo 1.0` depends on `bar`. A new version `bar 2.0` is released, and it _breaks_ `foo`. Nobody knew this at the time `foo` was built, of course, so they just used a bare, unversioned `bar` dependency. Unfortunately, this means that if users update `bar` in those environments, `foo` is going to break.

So what we'd like to do is modify the metadata `foo 1.0` so that it depends on `bar <2.0`. _None_ of the program code is changing, this is strictly a metadata change. If we do this for all _existing_ builds, then `conda` users get the benefit of this new metadata, and their environments will not break if they do `conda update bar`.

On the other hand, if we refuse to update existing builds, then those builds of `foo` are now _broken_ in the current conda universe. Anyone who does `conda install foo=1.0 bar=2.0`, for instance, is going to get the broken build of `foo`.

The following works:
```
#! /usr/bin/python                                                                                                                                                                                                                           

import pysam

with pysam.AlignmentFile('pysam_data/ex1.bam') as inf:

def split_PySam_test_by_chromosome(info):
    chromosome, length = info
    with pysam.AlignmentFile('pysam_data/ex1.bam') as inf:
        with pysam.AlignmentFile(
                "test_%s.bam" % chromosome,
                "wb",

            for alignment in inf.fetch(reference=chromosome):
                outf.write(alignment)

list(map(split_PySam_test_by_chromosome, chromosomes))
```
The issue has been that you are writing reads to BAMs with different sequence dictionaries. When writing reads from one BAM file to another you need to ensure that the sequence dictionary is the same, i.e. the same chromosomes appear and in the same order. This is because in the BAM format the chromosome a reads maps to is a number (tid) that needs to be consistent. Alternatively, the following works:
```
#! /usr/bin/python                                                                                                                                                                                                                           

import pysam

with pysam.AlignmentFile('pysam_data/ex1.bam') as inf:
    chromosomes = zip(inf.references, inf.lengths)

def split_PySam_test_by_chromosome(info):
    chromosome, length = info
    with pysam.AlignmentFile('pysam_data/ex1.bam') as inf:
        with pysam.AlignmentFile(
                "test_%s.bam" % chromosome,
                "wb",
                reference_names=[chromosome],
                reference_lengths=[length]) as outf:
            for alignment in inf.fetch(reference=chromosome):
                alignment.tid = 0
                outf.write(alignment)

list(map(split_PySam_test_by_chromosome, chromosomes))
```
As you can see, that is a bit cumbersome.
Unfortunately, as the header is written at the top of a BAM file, it is the header that determines how tids map to chromosome names, and not vice versa.

Please note also when multiprocessing that you can not use an already opened AlignmentFile object.

That's why tiered, named snapshots is my recommendation.  If both `rapid-TIMESTAMP` and `hourly-TIMESTAMP` refer to some of the same segments, then those segments will be referenced by a restore, even if `rapid-TIMESTAMP` is deleted later on.  This is both the blessing and the curse of snapshots.  The more you have, the more segment pointer checks have to be made, but the less storage space you consume, and the less network/shared-disk-i/o you have to worry about.

I'm not talking about automatically disabling shard rebalancing. I only mentioned that as a workaround to what I see as the underlying problem, and I agree that this is something that the user should explicitly perform.

However, if the user does not disable rebalancing, and still calls the shrink operation, that is an explicit indication that the user wants the two to occur simultaneously. This is the situation in which I mean that the shrink operation should not wait for other, unrelated indexes to recover before executing. Also, the `shrink` command will still be subject to the limits regarding per-node and cluster-wide simultaneous recoveries, so the user's safeguards regarding I/O and network bandwidth will still be observed.

@marvinpuethe that's a different error actually. You got past the `SSL: CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED` error but ran into a filesystem permissions problem.

It's difficult to know much about users, groups, filesystem permissions and the search `PATH` on either Windows or Linux, so `az` takes a hands-off approach by trying to write to the common `/usr/local/bin` location. In this case, your user didn't have permissions to write there, which isn't uncommon.

As a workaround, either run the command with `sudo` to give yourself the necessary permissions, or download the file to a writeable location and then move it into your search `PATH`:
```
sudo az aks install-cli  # or
az aks install-cli --install-location=./kubectl && sudo mv kubectl /usr/local/bin/kubectl
```

Hi @vcanaa, thanks for bringing up the issue, the documentation is wrong in this case. To clarify:
- if_match=* means "if etag matches anything", which basically translates to "if blob exists"
- if_none_match=* means "if etag does not match anything", which is "if blob does not exist". So to make sure you are not overriding an existing blob, you should use if_none_match=*.

I will update the documentation. Thank you!

> If I need to train on dozens of wav. files, which means to use the output transmat_ as the input for next training, still there's chance some of the elements of transmat_ may <= 1, how can I solve this?

You don't need `transmat_prior` to preserve the transmat between sequences. Here's what you can do insted:

* Use transmat from the previous sequence as a starting point for the next sequence. To do this drop `"t"` from `init_params` and set the `transmat_` attribute to the previously estimated one. 
* Fit the model to multiple sequences using the `length` argument. See "Working with multiple sequences" section in the [tutorial](http://hmmlearn.readthedocs.org/en/latest/tutorial.html#training-hmm-parameters-and-inferring-the-hidden-states).

Please give any of these a try and let me know if it worked.

> The 2nd question in my first comment happened during the training of the 2nd file. The covars_ of gmm start to drop below 0 when the error occurs.

`ValueError` means that one of the GMMs had nan in `covars_` matrix after the M-step. This could've been caused by using an improper (non positive semi-definite) covariance matrix in the E-step.

I've never used HMMs in both these settings. If you have a good reference, feel free to post it here, so we could consider implementing it in `hmmlearn` or submit a PR.

P.S. for the first problem you might want to look at [this](https://www.hds.utc.fr/~tdenoeux/dokuwiki/_media/en/publi/ps_hmm_conf.pdf) paper on partially observed HMMs.

FWIW, I do agree that cleaning is a mess.

Normally, this ends up being worse after any sort of testing stage as there really is no interest in keeping such a layer. Instead one is only interested in validating the previously layer works as expected and then continue/finish the build without any changes to the final layer caused by testing.

This is certainly not the first time I have seen this issue and felt I should raise it after our discussion here with particular regards to testing. I created an issue here ( https://github.com/docker/docker/issues/16993 ) if you are interested. There are some workarounds proposed relating to local builds and builds on Docker Hub. The real solution will likely rely on some sort of nested build implementation. There are many proposals the most notable being this one ( https://github.com/docker/docker/issues/7115 ). In addition to helping with testing, it seems this proposal might make it easier to avoid clean up at almost any `RUN` step by doing a nested build and only including the needed build artifacts.

However, as it is very versatile it will take some honing before it makes it into `master` or a release for that matter. Whether or not the final implementation uses something like `dind` would determine how versatile this solution would be. Currently, this sort of syntax change is blocked by the freezing of the `Dockerfile` syntax ( https://github.com/docker/docker/blob/master/ROADMAP.md#22-dockerfile-syntax ). FWICT, it is unclear when the `Dockerfile` syntax will be unfrozen.

In short, given the interest and people behind this change we can be fairly certain we will see something to alleviate this problem, but given the current blockers it won't be soon.

> > So, if the methods are going to stay, then I think we should ... explicitly forbid any field names that would cause a conflict
>
> I understand the motivation, but I think this would be unwise. Developers are not always in control of the schema that they use [...]

Yeah, this is in line with my initial idea that we'd free up the namespace by renaming the methods to `_keys` etc.

A counterargument is that the mapping interface comes in handy when calling a general-purpose function that has been designed to act on dictionaries. The schema issue is avoided by doing something like:

```python
class Foo(Model):
    keys_ = ListType(StringType, serialized_name='keys')

```

I've been warming up to the counterargument's viewpoint in principle, but the trouble is that a general-purpose function might also expect methods like `update` or `copy` or `setdefault`. Where do we stop?

One solution is simply to *allow* one to write general-purpose functions, not try to accommodate all existing functions out there. The sweet thing about this is that it can be accomplished without consuming the method namespace at all. To derive the full `Mapping` interface you only need `__getitem__`, `__iter__`, and `__len__`. The fundamental idiom is:

  ```python
  for k in obj:
    obj[k]
  ```

Since `keys()`, `values()`, and `items()` are just derivatives of the above, they're not strictly necessary.

Or, we could just keep doing what we do now: expose the standard mapping methods but allow developers to mask them with fields at their discretion. Maybe raise a suppressible warning if they do this. Out of the three options, this one's probably my least favorite. If we're going to support general-purpose functions, I don't like the idea of such a function suddenly breaking if a model defines an offending name.

> > I believe it would be more useful to iterate over name–value pairs.
>
> Agree. And this will ensure that `dict(instance)` works as expected, without the need for the complete mapping interface. If a user wants more control (roles, whether to include undefined values, etc), they can use `to_dict` which should provide more options.

The thing about the `dict` constructor is that it bases its behavior on the `keys` attribute on the source object. If `keys` exists, it must be an actual `keys()` method. So, as long as `"keys"` is a legal field name, then `dict()` is not reliable.

Is supporting `dict(instance)` necessary? In my view, no. All it does is create a poor man's copy of the instance, where the root becomes a dictionary but any submodels remain models. The right way to do it is via `copy.copy()` or `copy.deepcopy()`.

Thus, if `keys()` is going away, then I'd rather disable `dict(instance)` altogether so as to avoid a situation where it seemingly works... until your model defines a field named `"keys"`.

As for the iteration behavior, I have actually backtracked on the key–value idea in favor of dictionary compatibility. Some degree of mapping emulation seems desirable, and changing the iteration behavior is not really compatible with that.

It's been a while since I thought about this issue. The `BytearrayStream` object that we use for encodings is mutable. When the encoding gets read, it actually gets consumed by the reader. The bytes get pulled off the buffer and converted into whatever attribute or field is getting built at that moment, and the buffer shrinks. This makes it easy to track where we are in the buffer when reading; we're always at the beginning. When we use `is_tag_next`, we're peeking ahead into the buffer without pulling off bytes. When the reader is done reading/building itself, the buffer should be empty (that's what `is_oversized` checks for).

The simple fix is to just make a deep copy of the encoding and use that each time you want to read it in.

Long term, I've thought about changing how the `BytearrayStream` behaves (e.g., turning it into a static buffer). To be honest, it's not usually a problem. It's one of those library internal details that I just know and no one else thinks about.

> [...] any beamline can be considered as a reference and generic use case. Furthermore, everything (including the url address) is captured in the VCR file that can be generated by the simulation program without the EPICS Archiver.

That's true, but it doesn't solve the whole problem. Imagine this realistic situation: A collaborator from the APS makes a change that involves sending a different request to the Archiver. Thus, the relevant VCR "recording(s)" need to be updated. That collaborator will run the tests against their own PV(s) using their own Archiver deployment. When they commit the results, all the VCR recordings will be changed to reflect the APS PV(s). Later, when someone at NSLS-II makes a change, all the recordings will be changed back. This thrashing will accumulate large diffs in the git history over time.

It could be avoided by capturing the recordings against a standard Archiver deployment that anyone can stand up using a bash script. We could include that script to make it easy for others to contribute. Could you try following the [Archiver Quickstart](https://slacmshankar.github.io/epicsarchiver_docs/quickstart.html) instructions to see how much trouble it would be a run a standard deployment?

The VCR layer still has value because you'll be able to run the tests without any Archiver deployment as long you aren't changing the requests. That will lower the barrier to entry for the vast majority of contributions, and it will make CI simpler/faster.

Hi there,

interesting idea! Regarding your question, I think the easiest way to have generations is to simply have a generation attribute for each agent in your model. Depending on if self.generation == "child" or "adult" they can then learn, die or mature (interesting order you picked there ;) )
Classically this would be tied to an "age" attribute, but could also be implemented otherwise.

Another idea, if the actions of children and adults are very different and you clearly want to separate them, you could have two agent-types and simply transfer the attributes of the Child into a new Adult once the Child matures (and in model-terms kill the Child agent).

But maybe I am missing an important point why your model would need a new Scheduler or BatchRunner. You should ask yourself "what is time in this model?" and "what is a scheduled step in this model?". Maybe your ideas can be simplified to use the existing Schedulers?

It can be easily circumvent by using the `unicode-escape` encoder and then applying the `ascii` decoder.

So, it's not just you.  This is a bit of a confusing question.  The short answer, IIRC, is that there's no fully standard way to specify keytab location.  The GSSAPI RFC itself (2743 and 2743) don't really "know" about keytabs, since they're mostly a krb-specific implementation detail, and you can have other GSSAPI implementations.

The `KRB5_CLIENT_KTNAME` is a krb5-specific mechanism, but will work.  Later on, various implementations (namely, MIT krb5) added support for an unofficial extension for loading an storing from implementation-specific credential stores via the [Credential Store Extension](https://pythongssapi.github.io/python-gssapi/latest/gssapi.raw.html#module-gssapi.raw.ext_cred_store), not to be confused with [RFC 5588](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5588.html), which looks suspiciously like the credential store extension, but exists to get your hopes up about a standard way to do this, only to dash them against the rocks of a significantly more specific use case.

So, the credentials store extension is mostly what you want.  The problem is, the actual details of storing things are implementation-specific, which is why they're not really documented here (not so much as a matter of policy at this point, but that's a discussion we should have @frozencemetery).  A MIT krb5-specific example can be found in the unit tests: https://github.com/pythongssapi/python-gssapi/blob/master/gssapi/tests/test_high_level.py#L189.

Unfortunately, IIRC, the full structure of that dict is only really documented in the krb5 code itself, but  https://web.mit.edu/kerberos/krb5-1.12/doc/basic/ccache_def.html shows part of the structure.

Please let me know if you have any more questions.

@frozencemetery we might want to have an "ancillary docs" section that documents quirks of MIT krb5, since most people are using this with that.

@PatrickHeppler Probably because the install-cert-for-lighttpd script has no idea when the renewal will happen.

@bwesterb I think this is rather inelegant. The main selling point of Let's Encrypt is to make the life of admins easier. One way it does that is by automating the installation process for standard software. So there should not be a script that you have to give on the command line to make renewal work on standard software. #1425 is easily solvable by setting the group permissions of the certificates right – no script needed. For the automation on cert renewal you are probably right: We need some kind of hook that gets executed. But that should be a module in the letsencrypt client, not a script you have to write yourself and it should take into account that multiple applications use the certificate and therefore need to have their hooks executed. As the format is needed for multiple pieces of software it should be either generated unconditionally (which would make custom configurations for software where we do not have an installer module easier) or it should use a dependency based mechanism to generate the formats that are required by an installer module.

    407         elif dt.tzinfo is not self:
--> 408             dt = self.localize(dt, is_dst)
    409             return dt.tzinfo._utcoffset
    410         else:

pytz\tzinfo.pyc in localize(self, dt, is_dst)
    347         # ambiguous case

--> 349             raise AmbiguousTimeError(dt)
    350

AmbiguousTimeError: 2014-11-02 01:00:00
```
The reason that `.utcnow()` works is because it retrieves the *current* time in UTC. The issue is that `tzlocal()` doesn't know that the time you're trying to parse is the current time - even if it happens to match the current time's signature, the current time's signature, without a time zone, is ambiguous during the DST transition.

That said, we may well be able to do this better by overloading `fromutc()`, but `pytz` has no way of knowing what your local time zone is, and figuring out what your *actual* local time zone is (as opposed to inferring the current time zone from the values returned by `time` - which is what causes the ambiguity) is actually a bit more complicated than what we're doing here. It's worth looking into, but I am convinced that it's not trivial to fix.

import cloudpickle

import unittest.mock
unpatched = cloudpickle.CloudPickler.extract_func_data                  
def picklepatch(self, func):    
    code, f_globals, defaults, closure, dct, base_globals = unpatched(self, func)
    
    # check if any top-level dependency is an imported package
    for x in set(f_globals.values()) | set(closure): 
        if isinstance(x, cloudpickle.types.ModuleType) and x.__package__:
            # check if the package has any currently loaded sub-imports
            prefix = x.__name__ + '.'
            for name, module in cloudpickle.sys.modules.items():
                if name.startswith(prefix): 
                    # check whether the function can address the sub-module
                    tokens = set(name[len(prefix):].split('.'))
                    if not tokens - set(code.co_names):
                        self.save(module) # ensure the unpickler executes import of this submodule

Nested arrays sound useful. Can you add a call to `self._warn()` in the event that the array named doesn't exist yet? One reason I hadn't added nested arrays in the past is that RiveScript files should be loadable in any order, and if an array in file "A" embeds a nested array that was defined in file "B", then it won't work how the user expected if those files are loaded in the wrong order.

Defining all arrays that refer to each other inside the same file makes that problem a little easier, but the parser should emit a warning whenever it sees an embedded array that hasn't yet been defined.

Another complication with nested arrays will be with the `deparse()/write()` methods; after parsing, since all the sub-arrays were expanded out, the actual names of the sub-arrays get lost and can't be reproduced when written back to disk. These functions are for advanced use cases though so it's not the worst problem in the world.

Also what happens if the nested array contains more arrays itself?

```
! array primary = red green blue
! array secondary = magenta cyan yellow
! array monochrome = gray grey black white
! array rgb = @primary @secondary
! array colors = @rgb @monochrome orange brown pink
```

I think a more robust solution to all of the above problems will be to only expand nested arrays at reply matching time. So after parsing, the "colors" array above would contain literal `@rgb` type entries, and in the `_reply_regexp()` function use a `while` loop when processing arrays. So, if after processing one array you get new `@array` parts in the result, process it again; the `while` loop would run until there are no more `@array`, or the trigger text doesn't change from one loop to another, or when `self._depth` is reached (to give a warning and `break` when you have two arrays that reference each other in an infinite loop).

- - -

As for the Slash Commands, you should be able to just use them as-is when UTF-8 mode is enabled (`rs = RiveScript(utf8=True)`), which causes the syntax checker for triggers to be a lot looser.

I try to be consistent with my own terms, and hope that others adopt them, but time will tell.  The single diode equation is a model (in a general sense) for an IV curve but it is not a model for a photovoltaic device. The single diode equation has 5 coefficients (I prefer not to call them parameters, although I've sort of given up on making that distinction).  When I write 'single diode model' I mean the diode equation together with the other equations describing how the 5 coefficients change with irradiance, temperature, etc.  The constants appearing in this larger set of equations are the single diode model parameters, e.g., light current at STC, alpha, diode factor, etc.  E.g., the Desoto model actually has 8 parameters: IL, Io, n, Rsh and Rs at STC (5), alpha_Isc, Eg at STC and dEgdT at STC. (3 more parameters). 

In this nomenclature, Desoto is a photovoltaic performance model, PVsyst includes a photovoltaic performance  model, but the single diode (or n-diode) equation is not a PV performance model.

To comment on what @adriesse writes, the 3 steps are separate and each is important. The '5 parameters at reference conditions'.  Those 5 values are some, but not all, of the parameters for the Desoto, CEC or PVsyst performance models.  To summarize what is in pvlib-python for each of the 3 steps mentioned by @adriesse :
1) determine model parameters from measurements,  No capability currently in pvlib-python but there's a stale, unfinished pull request for a method to estimate PVsyst model parameters.
2) given parameters that determine a single diode performance model, calculate the 5 coefficients for the single diode equation at the desired irradiance etc. conditions.  Here pvlib-python has calcparams_desoto, we could add calcparams_PVsyst, calcparams_CEC, etc.
3) calculate an IV curve given a set of 5 coefficients.  This is the current singlediode function.

> Very interesting discussion.

Thanks a lot for chiming in! This is really helpful.

> You are right that we need to find a consensus about these formulations.

Either that, or offer multiple variants. If there is no consensus in literature, we are not in a position to change that. There also seems to be no consensus on whether to use Jaccard or Dice, so maybe we should offer both.

> the definition of 'union', 'intersection' and |A| in case of either A or B (or maybe even both) being non-binary. I think we should discuss these three separately.

Yes, good idea!

> If we consider 1) and 3) for intersect and union to be equivalent then there are only two alternatives left

I think we should not dismiss the case where both A and B are non-binary. Imagine you're trying to predict a non-binary mask, with a non-binary target (e.g., for spectrogram-based source separation).

> `union(A,B) = sum(A) + sum(B) - intersect(A,B)`

That's a good idea to obtain a consistent definition. Note that if `intersect(A,B) = sum(min(A,B))`, this leads to `union(A,B) = sum(A) + sum(B) - sum(min(A,B)) = sum(max(A,B))`.

Also note that your lineup doesn't discuss the definitions of `union` and `intersection`, but of `|union|` and `|intersection|`. We could also try to split this up. Then we have:

* jaccard = |intersect(A,B)| / |union(A,B)|
* dice = 2 * |intersect(A,B)| / (|A| + |B|)
* intersect(A,B):
  * min(A,B)
  * A*B
* union(A,B):
  * max(A,B)
* |A|:
  * sum(A)
  * sum(A^2)
  * sqrt(sum(A^2))

To cover all definitions we had before, we still need:
* |union(A,B)|:
  * |A| + |B| - |intersect(A,B)|
  * |A|^2 + |B|^2 - |intersect(A,B)|

The latter is needed for https://arxiv.org/abs/1701.03056. The `|union(A,B)| = sum(A^2) + sum(B^2)` definition I had in https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/pull/818#issuecomment-291927147 was incomplete, sorry -- they also have the `- |intersect(A,B)|` part in Eq 3 of https://arxiv.org/abs/1701.03056. The weird part is that they mix the squared and non-squared L2 norms in the denominator. It kind of makes sense if you assign a virtual unit to A and B, to have the virtual units of `sum(sqr(A))`, `sum(sqr(B))` and `sqrt(sum(sqr(A*B)))` match. I'm not sure what to make of this.

Now as I noted above, the `|union(A,B)|` definition in terms of `|A| + |B| + |intersect(A,B)|` recovers the `union(A,B) = max(A,B)` definition for `|A| = sum(A)` and `intersect(A,B) = min(A,B)`, so maybe there's no good reason to have `union(A,B) = max(A,B)` in the list. If we combine `union(A,B) = max(A,B)` with anything else than `|A| = sum(A)` and `intersect(A,B) = min(A,B)`, it will become inconsistent.

This leaves us with two choices for the intersection, three choices for the cardinality, and two choices for the union, as well as the choice between Jaccard and Dice.

Another way to see it would be to directly define `|intersect(A,B)|` (6 choices if we allow all combinations we had above, 4 choices if we avoid combining min(A,B) with L2, or more choices if we want to come up with something crazy), and then define `|union(A,B)| = |intersect(A,A)| + |intersect(B,B)| - |intersect(A,B)|` (for Jaccard) and `|A| + |B| = |intersect(A,A)| + |intersect(B,B)|` (for Dice). This would still exclude https://arxiv.org/abs/1701.03056.

Phew... is there anything we can argue against / rule out? I personally don't see why to use an L2 norm at all... for binary vectors, the squared L2 norm is the same as the L1 norm, but the square root seems to come out of the blue.

I think it would be good to have a separation between model container / training loop and dataset container / batch generator. 

The model would have `train` and `predict` (+`evaluate`?) methods, which can be passed either simple arrays / shared variables, or something that produces minibatches (a `Dataset` object). We could provide some that cover simple cases like a directory of images, but ideally the user would define a subclass to handle their own data loading (along with things like preprocessing or realtime augmentation).

> I was also thinking about supporting more complicated use cases, i.e. what if there are multiple input layers, or what if there is no label. Maybe we could transparently support those, while assuming the default case of inputs + labels. Perhaps users could specify some kind of mapping between the layers (or Theano variables) and the various bits of data that are passed in.

I think this would work pretty well just by having the `Model` constructor accept a list of output layers, and an optional list of input layers/variables.

I do think this would be a key addition to the library, but it could also become quite a mess trying to support every possible thing people may want (logging, web monitoring, callbacks for early stopping, checkpointing, etc.). I think it would be best to really focus on the most common cases, while making the code as clean and obvious as possible so that people can adapt it to their own needs.

> I will test it with initial learning rate 0.1 and initial momentum 0.9 which are the one used for the standard momentum algorithm.

Note that there are two possible formulations for the momentum algorithm:
1. velocity := velocity * momentum - eta * grad
2. velocity := velocity * momentum - eta * (1 - momentum) * grad

Lasagne uses the more classical first one, but if I read this code correctly, Torch uses the second one: https://github.com/torch/optim/blob/master/sgd.lua#L62
If that is true, it means hyperparameters in Torch-based papers will not match up with hyperparameters in many other papers (for a momentum of 0.9, the learning rate in a Torch paper will be 10 times as large).
You could try adding `* (1 - updates[mu])` to the learning rate in https://gist.github.com/botev/f8b32c00eafee222e47393f7f0747666#file-yellow_fin-py-L62. Although I'm not sure if YellowFin is actually sensitive to that?

I had a similar issue. The default behaviour for column names is to use the field name but I needed the field's `verbose_name` instead.

To do this I just replaced the `field_from_django_field` method changing the section in bold below:

```
class ContentResource(resources.ModelResource):
    @classmethod
    def field_from_django_field(self, field_name, django_field, readonly):
        """
        Returns a Resource Field instance for the given Django model field.
        """

        FieldWidget = self.widget_from_django_field(django_field)
        widget_kwargs = self.widget_kwargs_for_field(field_name)
        field = fields.Field(attribute=field_name, **column_name=django_field.verbose_name**,
                widget=FieldWidget(**widget_kwargs), readonly=readonly)
        return field
```

It seems to me that it's a bug in `sqlalchemy`? The `mssql` module imports the `TIMESTAMP` type but according to the [docs](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/ms182776%28v=SQL.90%29.aspx) it isn't the same type as the ANSI SQL TIMESTAMP.

i.e. `isinstance(sa.TIMESTAMP(), mssql.TIMESTAMP)` *should* fail

Manually patching my sqlalchemy:
```patch
--- lib/sqlalchemy/dialects/mssql/base.py
+++ lib/sqlalchemy/dialects/mssql/base.py
@@ -617,13 +617,18 @@ from ... import engine
 from ...engine import reflection, default
 from ... import types as sqltypes
 from ...types import INTEGER, BIGINT, SMALLINT, DECIMAL, NUMERIC, \
-    FLOAT, TIMESTAMP, DATETIME, DATE, BINARY,\
+    FLOAT, DATETIME, DATE, BINARY,\
     TEXT, VARCHAR, NVARCHAR, CHAR, NCHAR
 
 
 from ...util import update_wrapper
 from . import information_schema as ischema
 
+
+# The mssql TIMESTAMP value is *not* the ANSI SQL TIMESTAMP type
+# https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/ms182776%28v=SQL.90%29.aspx
+TIMESTAMP = BINARY
+
 # http://sqlserverbuilds.blogspot.com/
 MS_2016_VERSION = (13,)
 MS_2014_VERSION = (12,)
-- 
```

@app.route('/')
def index():
    ...
```

I imagine others may think that is the natural approach, for example, they may have a package where the \__init__.py creates the vehicle object, then imports the attribute/message listeners which are top-level functions in a sub-module. Perhaps:

drone/\__init__.py
```python
vehicle = connect(...)

import script

drone/script.py
```python
from drone import vehicle

@vehicle.on_attribute('location')
def listener(...)
    ...
```
