One other comment.  If you remove the last stage detail `coeffs` the reconstructed volume will be smaller than the input as the 6-level transform will be truncated to 5-levels.  If you instead set it to an empty dict, the final stage will be run as if all detail coefficients were zeros, but due to a subtle implementation detail that relies on shapes of both the approx and detail coefficients to determine how to appropriately shape the output, the array may be slightly larger than the original input by some small padding amount.  The only workaround I see for this is as in the final case below:

```python
import numpy as np
import pywt

cam = pywt.data.camera().astype(np.float32)  # shape (512, 512)

c = pywt.wavedecn(cam, 'db4', level=6, mode='constant')

if False:
    # remove finest detail coefficients (results in a 5 level inverse transform
    c = c[:-1]
    cr_256 = pywt.waverecn(c, 'db4', mode='constant')
elif False:
   # remains a 6-level transform, but as if all detail coeffs at the finest detail were zeros
    c[-1] = {}
    cr_514 = pywt.waverecn(c, 'db4', mode='constant')
    # shape is (514, 514), not (512, 512) as expected!
    cr_512 = cr_514[:-2, :-2]  # have to manually truncate output to the original shape
else:
    c[-1] = {k: np.zeros_like(v) for k, v in c[-1].items()}
    cr_512 = pywt.waverecn(c, 'db4', mode='constant')
    # now size (512, 512) as expected
```

I have defined a `DatabaseId` interface

```
class DatabaseId(graphene.Interface):
    db_id = Int()
```

`Class User` is an implementation of that interface. I then map the database id as `db_id` and used only_fields to select the fields I want.

```
class User(SQLAlchemyObjectType):
    class Meta:
        model = UserModel
        interfaces = (relay.Node, DatabaseId)
        model.db_id = model.id
        only_fields = [
            'id', 'othernames', 'surname', 'email'
        ]
```

Hey @yurishkuro thanks for the review.

So the idea is to have something slightly less fragile than what we have now, and have people fetch the global `Tracer` themselves if they want - for reference, in `opentracing/__init__.py` it's written:

```python
# Note: it should be accessed via 'opentracing.tracer', not via
# 'from opentracing import tracer', the latter seems to take a copy.
tracer = Tracer()
```

So with this approach, users could just grab it and forget about this detail (if they want) ;) Of course, the advantages may not be as many as what we have now, thus the call for feedback.

@bensigelman 

 1. Not sure I understand the scenario. If tracer lib imports v.0.8 of opentracing API and extends the Tracer, it inherits the method with the correct version. Are we saying that the end user app has v.0.9 opentracing lib, but tracer impl written against 0.8? If that's the case, we can make the method abstract in the opentracing tracer, but that's not going to protect against implementor forgetting to bump the version. And I would also think the tracer's dependency should be `<0.9` in this case.

 2. I meant that exposing capabilities via version seems like a very fragile method. I can't imagine how ugly the client code will look doing those checks. The usual approach to combat API version changes is to forward or backport the API via a shim. E.g. if the instrumentation is done via v.0.9 but the bound tracer is only v.0.8 than the tracer should be wrapped in a decorator..

But I have to admit that I haven't had the pleasure of writing such spaghetti code in dynamic langauges and checking versions, so I don't want to `-1` this if people feel strongly that it's needed.

Hi @FireBrother. That's good to hear; this is almost certainly the cause of the issue.

When you say that you followed the instructions in that issue to build TensorFlow, does that include checking out `db596594b5653b43fcb558a4753b39904bb62cbd~`? That is a relatively old tree, from 2017-08-02, and in particular it is six days before the change in which we made `SummaryMetadata.plugin_data` a non-repeated field. You can confirm that in the source here: https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/b9ac2d7eb17022a677597a1f88a65d8d26278088/tensorflow/core/framework/summary.proto#L53

So the problem is simply that you are running incompatible versions of TensorBoard and TensorFlow.

Some possibilities:
  * Is it possible to build TensorFlow with cudnn7.0 support against HEAD? This would be the best option.
  * Is it possible to build TF+cudnn7.0 against 4c60c96257bfd54a036d15af979e90fc0b4e400d?
  * If neither of the above two is possible, it should certainly be possible to build TF+cudnn7.0 against `db596594b5653b43fcb558a4753b39904bb62cbd~` but with `4c60c96257bfd54a036d15af979e90fc0b4e400d` cherry-picked in, and this should be sufficient resolve the issue (at the risk of using a TensorFlow codebase that was never actually the state of the repository at any time).
  * If for some reason that is not possible either, you'll have to downgrade TensorBoard to 0.1.2, I think.

In theory, everything should work under Windows, but I expect that everything will be much simpler if you use a different operating system. (For instance, Bazel's Windows support is "experimental," and you certainly need Bazel.)

An alternative to doing this is to bump the version only when you make the new release. This has the nice side-effect that you can set the tag to point to a commit that bumps the version. What do you prefer?

Is it really an issue? Like you've said, lets assume:
1. `Client::ProcessReceivedMessage()` - ignores CON/NON request messages, i.e. processes CON/NON with response code, ACK and RST messages.
2. `Server::ProcessReceivedMessage()` - ignores CON/NON response messages, i.e. processes CON/NON with request code, ACK and RST messages.

If either server or client receives unknown ACK/RST message, they simply ignore it.
If CON/NON could not be handled then at most only one RST would be sent, because either a client would generate a RST if `msg.code >= kCoapResponseCodeMin` OR a server if `msg.code < kCoapResponseCodeMin`.

The comment explains why we can't do that. We use xlrd to read XLS files. Excel doesn't have an integer type. [Here is a list of all the types](https://xlrd.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#xlrd.sheet.Cell).

The only way to get `4.0` to look like `4` is to implement Excel's [custom number formats](https://support.office.com/en-us/article/Create-or-delete-a-custom-number-format-78f2a361-936b-4c03-8772-09fab54be7f4), which can be very complex, and which contain irrelevant information for CSV conversion like whether negative numbers are red, whether to include a currency symbol, etc. And after formatting, what you'd get at the end is a string, not an integer, because format codes can produce character sequences that are not native Python integers.

That fix works only because `inst->constructed = true`, which is already there, doesn't actually do anything unless `inst->owned` is also true.  I think it should also go into the "variant 2" init_holder_helper so that returning a shared_ptr also does the right thing.

But I think instead there's something confusing in this logic in `class_::dealloc`:

* if we own the pointer:
  * if we constructed a holder, invoke its destructor
  * otherwise delete it via `delete`

It seems to me that the logic *should* be:

* if we have constructed a holder around the pointer, invoke the holder's destructor
* otherwise, if we own the pointer, delete it via `delete`

But if I make that change I start getting double-frees.  Alternatively--and effectively the same as changing the logic since this is the only place `->constructed = true` is ever set--I can add `->owned = true` to all three `init_holder_helper` variants.  For the first two, everything tests fine, but when adding it to the third I start getting the same double-frees.  But that suggests to me that something else is wrong: we're constructing a non-copyable holder to hold a pointer (whenever we hit variant 3, i.e. *usually* since `unique_ptr` hits this one), but, at least in some cases, it looks like we never actually invoke that holder's destructor (unless I'm missing something).

So I *think* the proper fix here is for `class_::dealloc` to invoke the holder destructor whenever `inst->constructed` (without requiring `inst->owned`): but that's currently triggering another problem related to something being fishy in the non-copyable holder case.

Hey Thomi

tl;dr I think we should probably do this. Comes down to pythons "consenting adults" idea really, and I think not violating the principle of least surprise here is probably important.

The implementation is a little nuanced, so if you could answer some questions, that would help get it right. Honest feedback appreciated!

1) Just checking you are aware of the DEBUGLOG env var http://talisker.readthedocs.io/en/latest/logging.html#debug-logging. Thoughts on this?

2) Why specifically do you want debug output in the console rather than a file?

3) Do you set the DEVEL env var when doing local development? http://talisker.readthedocs.io/en/latest/logging.html#development. Did you know about this? Do you think it is useful/confusing/etc?

FWIW, never sending debug level logging to stderr is designed to prevent both information leakage and excessive volume in prod. We can relax this for sure in dev, but want to avoid making it easy to accidentally do this in production. E.g, one option would be to require DEVEL env var for it to work. Or we could just remove the restriction altogether iff --log-level=debug for gunicorn.

Certbot runs into problems when selecting which Apache VirtualHost to use as a base for the validation because currently only one of the VirtualHosts matching the requested domain name is selected. For reference, the selection is handled here: https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot-apache/certbot_apache/configurator.py#L589-L648

The (only) proper fix here would be to configure _all_ the matching VirtualHosts to be able to answer the HTTP challenge. Albeit localhost IP being a special case, it still doesn't mean that it's a wrong one to handle the challenge even in complex multi-VirtualHost configurations. There might be reverse proxy scheme that's effectively serving the requests from public internet by upstreaming the localhost ip, and the other matching VirtualHosts could be used to serve the internal network for example.

That being said, the current selection method is flawed, as it practically just configures the [first matching vhost](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot-apache/certbot_apache/configurator.py#L634-L636) for the validation.

As a rule of thumb: A `NewType` makes sense where you want to semantically differentiate something that you can not differentiate based on the raw shape of it. The canonical example is an API that wants to consume a distance in *miles* which, without a `NewType` is usually represented as an `int`. Calling this API with a distance in *kilometers* will obviously yield faulty results but the type system can't help you as it has no idea what kind of `int` the method should accept. 

I find `URI` a good example (in fact `URI` exists as a proper type in many languages anyway). I think `ContractName` is a bit more controversial but I think we can just try to be restrictive and see how it feels.

Hi @dstreppa , 

We currently rely on a smart contract called [`EndpointRegistry`](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/7e24e229e9bf58f9975334b937fde3579d1e21f0/raiden/smart_contracts/EndpointRegistry.sol) to map an ethereum address to an public ip/port pair.

If the ethereum node used by one of the raiden nodes is lagging behind it may query an older block and get and old ip/port entry, the partner raiden node is online but seems unreachable because the wrong ip/port pair is used. The state will stay at unreachable until the ethereum node synchronizes and the endpoint reports the up-to-date ip/port pair. Were you using two different ethereum nodes, could be the case the one of this nodes is lagging behind?

If you want to check what is IP/port reported by the ethereum node, right after the node is started you will see a log line printed to the screen like this:

```
INFO:raiden.network.upnpsock    registered port-mapping per upnp internal=192.168.15.164:40001 external=<public_ip>:40001
```

Then you can execute the following line from the console to query the end point registry:

```python
>>> discovery.get('2e960068875b8b3c4814ccc35fe35963f6556240'.decode('hex'))
```

If the values don't match that is the problem. There is a PR in the making that will check that the etheruem node is synced to fix this https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/pull/882.

If each node sees the correct endpoint, the problem then is on the network. Could you provide a little bit more detail about your network? I believe no one in the raiden team actually tested with virtualbox machines, either the nat punching is not working (we use [UPnP IGD](https://github.com/miniupnp/miniupnp) and [stun](https://github.com/jtriley/pystun) for this), or many packets are dropped by the network..

Hi,

Thanks for the PR. But I think that this solution is very specific to certain architectures.
As mentioned in https://github.com/pytorch/vision/issues/173#issuecomment-305737852 , I think a better solution would be something in the lines of

```python
def _update_with_pretrained_weights(model, model_path):
    model_dict = model.state_dict()
    pretrained_dict = torch.load(model_path)

    # 1. filter out unnecessary keys
    pretrained_dict = {k: v for k, v in pretrained_dict.items() if k in model_dict}
    # 2. overwrite entries in the existing state dict
    model_dict.update(pretrained_dict)
    # 3. load the new state dict
    model.load_state_dict(model_dict)
```

I could reproduce the same error with a pre-existing database from before the reorganization, where migrations 0001 ­– 0004 were applied with `default` as the appname.

After running `update django_migrations set app='social_django' where app='default';` in a `dbshell`, `showmigrations` is capable of finishing successfully, but it still shows all `social_django` migrations as not applied (even though the first four are already applied). In this state, obviously, running `migrate` leads to an error, because it tries to apply all of them from the beginning, but the tables are already there.

The solution (in my situation) is to run `migrate --fake social_django 0004`, which adds entries to `django_migrations` for all already-applied migrations for appnames `default` and `social_auth` as well. So in the end, I have entries in the `django_migrations` table for all of the already-applied migrations for all three appnames: `default`, `social_auth`, and `social_django`. At this point migrations work correctly for me again.

I did some more digging, and it looks like the problem here is that the `replaces` attribute of a Django migration has a different meaning than what is assumed in the `social_django` package. This does not appear to be documented (or at least, I didn't find it in the docs), but https://github.com/django/django/blob/f582db1296cc18e2ff49846c9be4ad999759adcc/django/db/migrations/migration.py#L39-L42 sheds a little bit of light on the problem.

In short, the way `replaces` is used in this package seems to be along the lines of “Consider this migration applied if any of these two original migrations has been applied before,” whereas the actual meaning as understood by Django is “This migration is a replacement for the entire sequence of these two original migrations.” So, if Django only sees one of the original migrations being applied, but not the other one, it assumes the replacement is not yet applied either. And conversely, when the replacement is being applied, all of the originals get recorded, too, because there might be some other migrations elsewhere that depend on some of the originals. Which also means that if any of the original migrations is missing in the database, the replacement cannot be considered applied either.

Not sure what the way forward would be, though. I guess removing one of the two entries in `replaces` in all migrations would be necessary, and also documenting that a manual `migrate --fake` will be required in certain situations. As far as I understand, Django does not provide any more straight-forward automatic upgrade path for situations like this (but I may be wrong).

I had a tricky time migrating, however I managed to overcome it using the following method to upgrade from python-social-auth v 0.2.19
1. upgrade to python-social-auth v 0.2.21

2. run ```python migrate social_auth```
Then your django_migrations table will look like this
```
default     | 0001_initial
default     | 0002_add_related_name
default     | 0003_alter_email_max_length
default     | 0004_auto_20160423_0400
social_auth | 0005_auto_20160727_2333
social_auth | 0001_initial
social_auth | 0003_alter_email_max_length
social_auth | 0004_auto_20160423_0400
social_auth | 0002_add_related_name
```

3. Run ```pip install social-auth-app-django>=1.0.0```
Use version >= 1.0.0 as I had some issues with v 0.0.1 - django was giving me a warning
```You have model changes without migrations blah....```

4. Update your project settings and imports under heading 'Settings' here https://github.com/omab/python-social-auth/blob/master/MIGRATING_TO_SOCIAL.md#migrations

5. Run all migrations ```python manage.py migrate'''

Now it should be working with all migrations applied.

@loboque First find your corresponding python site-packages location(in windows usually Users/username/AppData/Roaming/Python/PythonXX/site-packages or \AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python36\Lib\site-packages, in linux usually /usr/lib/pythonXX/site-packages/). then find the streamlink folder, download the following files into streamlink/plugins/ to replace the origin one. then it will work.
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/fozzysec/streamlink/cc06ca9f4deb13a1bc8b21a13b46986d92c8ad9f/src/streamlink/plugins/douyutv_blackbox.py
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/fozzysec/streamlink/ce234fc0ce9f74a3dab997db94f62dec85c1bbec/src/streamlink/plugins/douyutv.py

You can also download the project source code, place the files under src/streamlink/plugins/, then run `pythonXX setup.py install ` to install it.

I've started working on implementing this for async_generator, and the biggest wrinkle I've run into is that native async generators don't support returning values other than None. Combined with the conversion of explicitly raised StopAsyncIteration exceptions into RuntimeErrors, this means there's (AFAICT) no way to make an @async_generator, implemented as above as a native async generator awaiting an async function that yields magic wrapper objects, return something. This breaks a few of the tests, mostly of `yield_from_`. I wonder, though... do we care? Is anyone actually using the fact that async generators can return values? In the long run, does the async_generator project want to support things that Py3.6 doesn't support natively, or does it just want to provide a way for async-generator-using code to be Py3.5 compatible?

Ah, I see the problem. In virtualenv's own setup.py, only the setuptools egg for the current Python version is actually installed to `site-packages as part of `package_data`. If you were using `--distribute`, you wouldn't have this problem, because distribute is installed from a non-Python-version-specific sdist tarball. Virtualenv is looking in all the right places, but the setuptools egg for Python 2.7 wasn't installed there, only the one for 2.6.

I think, given the existence of the `--python` option, we should just install all of the setuptools eggs unconditionally, so that a single installation of virtualenv can be used to create virtualenvs on any supported Python version using `--python`, without having to download.

Thanks for tracking this down. The pull request should be fairly simple (just a patch to `setup.py` to include all `*.egg` in `package_data`, just like `*.tar.gz` is currently included, rather than the `sys.version` check) - if you can test this to make sure it fixes your problem and then submit a pull request, I'll merge it.

> I thought that the executable script location was standard, but I guess 
> I am wrong. I wonder how does pip know where to put the ipython 
> executable when one runs `pip insall ipython` then.

Pip delegates the actual installation of files to distutils; it uses the
install schemes built into Python and available in the sysconfig module,
which are based in part on the value of sys.prefix.

> As you can see, it explicitly refers to a particular interpeter so it 
> won't be possible to call it from the virtual environment of a different 
> interpreter.

Actually, it'll work just fine if you call it while you have a
virtualenv based on a different interpreter activated, but it won't run
within the virtualenv.

I understand your use case, but I still don't believe that virtualenv
should take it upon itself to automatically add paths outside the
virtualenv to the shell path. You can use virtualenvwrapper and
postactivate hook scripts to take care of your specific case.
`--relocatable` isn't so experimental for us either. Works great on standard configurations.
